title: Australia open to playing Test against England in India
description: England and Australia should be open to the idea of playing a Test match in India to help generate interest in the format, says the chief executive of Cricket Australia.
fullText:
England and Australia should be open to the idea of playing a Test match in India to help generate interest in the format, says the chief executive of Cricket Australia.

The two sides traditionally only meet in Test cricket as part of an Ashes series, which is played on a home-and-away basis, with each country hosting it roughly every four years.

Australia and England are, though, poised to play a one-off Test at the Melbourne Cricket Ground in March 2027 to celebrate the 150th anniversary of the first meeting between the sides.

And Cricket Australia chief executive Todd Greenberg told BBC Sport the prospect of taking international cricket's most storied - and marketable - Test rivalry to India should be on the table.

Such a fixture would need buy-in from both the England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB) and the Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI).

"It's not something that we are planning, but it's open for consideration. We'd have to be open to considering all options," Greenberg said.

"Our relationship with the ECB and the BCCI is very strong and clearly there are opportunities in India to put more content in certain areas."

Cricket Australia is already looking to expand its footprint in India by taking the Big Bash League overseas for the first time, with Melbourne Renegades due to face Perth Scorchers in Chennai in December.

The governing body says the move is designed to grow the competition's global appeal by tapping into cricket's biggest market.

The popularity of Test matches in India have declined in the modern era amid a diet of T20 and the dominance of the Indian Premier League.

Greenberg said there was "no doubt" an England v Australia Test played on Indian soil would capture the imagination.

"I think Test cricket between us, India and England is very strong and probably bucking the trend in some other parts of the world. We want to make sure we grow and strengthen it," he said.

Greenberg defended the decision to stage the 150th anniversary Test against England under lights.

The centenary Test in 1977 was also played in Melbourne but this match will be the first men's Test at the venue to use a pink ball.

Some high-profile former Australia players are understood to have voiced their disapproval to Cricket Australia but Greenberg said it was not his job "to be popular".

"We wanted to get as many people for the game as possible. Playing that game in the day while people are at work and school would've made that difficult," he said.

"Clearly there's a commercial imperative with broadcast rights back in the UK and the time zone. Pink-ball Tests for us are hugely advantageous in terms of audience.

"It will anger some of the traditionalists but we're in the business of getting more eyeballs on our game."

Greenberg said Cricket Australia was trying to strike a balance between marking a significant milestone in the history of Test cricket and ensuring the longer format continued to move with the times.

He also indicated preparations for the match would produce a pitch offering a better spectacle than the one used in last year's Ashes Test at the MCG which England won inside two days.

"We're trying to balance celebrating 150 years of Test cricket between our two countries but also have an eye on the future and how we can evolve the sport," added Greenberg.

"We're trying to find that balance and hopefully the MCG pitch will be a little flatter than it was for the last Ashes.

"We certainly do have a good record in pink-ball Tests, but I think you'll find this one will be a bit flatter and have less grass on it, that's the intention."

Listen to the full interview with Cricket Australia chief executive Todd Greenberg on the BBC Stumped podcast.

No 2027 men's Ashes Test in north of England

Ex-Australia batter David Warner pleads guilty to drink driving

Is Test cricket financially sustainable?

title: Rangers sign 'huge potential' in Partizan captain Dragojevic
description: Rangers sign 20-year-old Serbia midfielder Vanja Dragojevic from Partizan for an undisclosed fee.
fullText:
Rangers have signed Serbia midfielder Vanja Dragojevic from Partizan Belgrade for an undisclosed fee.

The 20-year-old has signed a four-year-contract with the Scottish Premiership club, with the option for a further year.

Dragojevic, who has two caps for his country, captained Partizan after coming through their youth ranks.

"We are really excited to bring Vanja on board," said manager Derek McInnes.

"He's got huge potential, really exciting attributes, and boasts the character to play in this environment.

"To captain a club the size of FK Partizan at such a young age tells you everything you need to know about the respect he commands and the leadership qualities he already possesses.

"I believe he will add a lot to our group, and I look forward to working with him in the years ahead."

Who has your Premiership club brought in & let go this summer?

Having joined Partizan's youth system in 2021, Dragojevic signed a professional contract two years later and was sent on loan to third-tier Teleoptik.

Within four months, he signed a new deal until 2030 with a reported release clause of £12.75m and made his first-team debut for Partizan in March last year.

He became a regular starter and captain last season, scoring four times in 41 appearances and providing four assists, while playing mainly as a defensive midfielder as Partizan finished third in the Serbian SuperLiga.

Dragojevic played in both legs of Partizan's Conference League third qualifying round defeat by Hibernian, when the Serbian side won 3-2 at Easter Road despite finishing with 10 men, but went out 4-3 on aggregate after extra time.

He made his international debut as a second-half substitute as Cape Verde warmed up for the World Cup finals with a 3-0 win over Serbia in May, before he started in a 5-1 thrashing by tournament co-hosts Mexico.

title: Hull City owner 'disturbed' by transfer claims
description: Hull City owner Acun Ilicali says he is "disturbed" by claims of the club being rejected by transfer targets and, in an unusual move, attempted to clarify the situation.
fullText:
Hull City owner Acun Ilicali has said he is "disturbed" by claims of the club being rejected by transfer targets and, in an unusual move, attempted to clarify the situation.

The Tigers are returning to the Premier League after a nine-year absence following their victory in the Championship play-off final in May.

So far, they have signed keeper Jack Butland from Rangers, midfielder Oscar Zambrano from Maribor and defender Matt Targett following his exit from Newcastle United as they prepare for the 2026-27 top-flight campaign.

"We are reading dozens of false transfer rumors in many places," wrote Ilicali in a social media post., external

"We are disturbed by the claims that we have been rejected and by the use of our club's name to gain attention.

"We have been working intensively for two months. I believe we will build a team that we will all be proud of."

Ilicali, who did not mention the reports he was referring to, added: "To prevent false speculation and to give you more information, I am leaving you with our latest transfer situation, which includes some clues..."

In the rest of his message, Ilicali numerically listed Butland, Zambrano and Targett with ticks next to their name.

He went on to put numbers from four to 11 with the flags of countries and percentages next to them as he suggested how close Hull were to signing players of those nations.

At number four he posted the Japan flag with 99%, and BBC Radio Humberside sports editor Mike White has reported that the Tigers have agreed a deal to sign free agent midfielder Hidemasa Morita.

Among the others, flags of Greece and Sweden had 90% next to them, while those of Australia, Croatia and Portugal were accompanied by 80%.

Hull City boss Sergej Jakirovic stated earlier this month that the Tigers were close to completing a deal for Olympiakos goalkeeper Konstantinos Tzolakis.

Ilicali recently said the club had taken out a £55m bridging loan to be in a "strong financial position" and help with completing transfers.

title: Alonso optimistic about Aston Martin upgrade - will it decide his future?
description: Fernando Alonso says he is "optimistic" about the major upgrade Aston Martin are introducing at the Hungarian Grand Prix.
fullText:
Fernando Alonso says he is "optimistic" about the major upgrade Aston Martin are introducing at the Hungarian Grand Prix, but has cautioned against expecting too much.

Aston Martin are introducing a heavily revised car, which features less weight, including a new chassis, and a significant aerodynamic change.

It is their first upgrade of the year after starting the season with a car and engine well below the best.

Alonso said: "Budapest is a friendly circuit for us. Power is not the most important here, but we update the car and not the engine yet.

"I am more into the weekend, understanding the direction of the car, if we are in a good path and we can unlock finally the performance."

Aston Martin have chosen to introduce their new parts all in one go rather than the more usual tactic of drip-feeding smaller upgrades over a number of races.

This was done as the team decided it was the most efficient use of their budget within the sport's spending cap.

But it means Aston Martin have progressively fallen further behind the field.

Russell proud of resilience after string of problems

How to follow Hungarian Grand Prix on the BBC

The last two circuits, Silverstone and Spa, have been particularly difficult for them because of the lack of performance of their Honda engine.

But even at the Spanish Grand Prix last month, a more corner-dependent track, they were four seconds off pole position, and more than three seconds off the Racing Bulls and Audi teams at the front of the midfield.

That is partly why Alonso is resisting the temptation to give any kind of judgement on where the term might end up this weekend.

"I am not thinking on numbers or positions," he said. "In the last five races we have very different gaps to the midfield.

"The other teams opted for a more conventional upgrade path, bringing 0.1-0.2secs every few races and then you end up with a second in race 12.

"We didn't do that and we try to catch up all in one go. It doesn't change your natural position in the end."

The Honda engine is not changing this weekend - the Japanese company is taking a development engine to the next race, the Dutch Grand Prix, after Formula 1's four-week summer break.

Aston Martin chief technical officer Adrian Newey said at the British Grand Prix earlier this month: "It became quite obvious very quickly that we were not going to be competitive in the early races.

"So, we took the painful but I believe correct decision to not do any developments through the first half of the year, knowing that that would actually mean, as everybody else develops, of course, the gap to the front would actually get bigger.

"But, with a view to then really getting ourselves better organised, putting lots of different systems into place for the future, and then really doing our research properly, because the '26-car was a very competitive timescale.

"So, it's enabled us to step back a bit, take a bit of pressure off ourselves, because I think we actually put ourselves under too much pressure over the winter, and take a deep breath, and really understand our problems, what we need to achieve, (with) this upgrade package that we go to Hungary as the first stage, the second stage in Zandvoort."

Alonso, who is 45 on 29 July, is out of contract at the end of the season and is weighing up whether to continue in F1.

He has said that he is committed to Aston Martin for the long term whether he stays in F1 or not, and he repeated that on Thursday, saying: "I do have a contract, so I'm not moving."

But he said his decision as to whether to keep racing in F1 would not be based solely on how the upgrade performed.

He is also undecided on whether the high levels of energy management needed with the latest generation of F1 engine is satisfying for him as a driver.

This issue is exacerbated on high-speed circuits such as Spa and Silverstone, whereas this weekend in Hungary there is expected to be no engine management because the circuit is slower and the engines will not be starved of energy.

"I have a pretty clear picture of the team that we will fix all the problems," Alonso said.

"They take time but I have no doubts Adrian will provide the best car on the grid sooner or later. I would like to see the first steps with this package.

"And the PU (power unit), I have no doubts, it is a matter of time before Honda fixes the issues.

"I am thinking I need to know what I do next year and the years to come. I feel fresh, motivated and fast but I need to enjoy what I do.

"Silverstone and Spa were not fun to drive. It is not fun to watch or drive. We have been saying the same thing, and it is not to criticise, it is to help everyone improve.

"The direction we took this year is not the same adrenaline I had before from an F1 car. It is a problem (as to whether) F1 gives me the adrenaline I need to live."

Why was Hamilton penalised but not Leclerc? - F1 Q&A

title: Can Emery work his magic again at Villa?
description: Aston Villa are back in the Champions League but must now cope with the loss of key players Morgan Rogers and Youri Tielemans.
fullText:
Unai Emery has transformed Aston Villa once. The question now is whether he can do it again.

Emery has got Villa to overperform for most of his three-and-a-half years in charge but has to cope with a summer of change at Villa Park. That has already begun and more is expected.

Villa have sold Morgan Rogers to Chelsea for £117m - a record move for an English player - a week after Youri Tielemans joined Manchester United.

Lucas Digne is expected to join Paris St-Germain and Villa could still have their resolve tested by Arsenal for Ezri Konsa.

Despite these squad changes, Villa will still be seeking another top-four finish next season - when the Premier League landscape looks certain to be a lot different from last season.

Big-spending Tottenham will expect a resurgence, Xabi Alonso will be aiming to revive Chelsea, Liverpool will hope to be revitalised under Andoni Iraola and Manchester United will believe Michael Carrick's good work as interim can continue.

So after ending their 30-year wait for a trophy with May's Europa League win, can Emery continue to produce his magic?

There was a sense at Villa Park last season they were getting into a party they were not invited to by qualifying for the Champions League.

Villa gatecrashed the top five for the second time in three seasons, finishing fourth to comfortably secure entry to club football's elite competition for 2026-27.

Emery has achieved this after inheriting a squad that was 14th in the table, three points above the relegation zone, in November 2022.

Villa's revival has happened despite having to sell, with for example Jacob Ramsey joining Newcastle for £40m last summer, to ensure compliance with financial rules.

Uefa's Squad Cost Ratio limits the amount clubs spend on transfers, wages and agent fees to 70% of their revenue. These financial shackles have long been a frustration for Villa.

Selling Rogers was the easiest way to comply with the regulations but it means large-scale spending to replace the England forward as well as Tielemans and Digne is unlikely without further significant exits.

Fringe players will leave though. Hull are keen on Leon Bailey but that may have to be an initial loan given the Tigers' own finances, while Evann Guessand can also depart. Tammy Abraham is however happy at Villa Park and not looking to leave.

Villa would have preferred to keep Tielemans but could not afford to renegotiate his deal, which had two years left, before United triggered his £35m release clause.

That is at least pure profit in the books after his free transfer arrival from Leicester City in 2023.

With Tielemans and Rogers gone and with Digne poised to follow, Villa have lost 489 Premier League appearances from the squad - plus two of the players who scored in the Europa League final.

Freiburg were poor that night in Istanbul but that did not reflect negatively on Johan Manzambi, who had caught Villa's eye more than a year before.

He joined for a club record fee of more than £50m just two years after making his senior debut for Freiburg in what was a case of now or never.

Villa were expecting the 20-year-old to move this summer - they hijacked a transfer to Newcastle - and had they not signed him, he would have been out of their reach in the future.

Manzambi, crowned Europa League Young Player of the Year, represents the start of a new era along with Joao Gomes following his £38m move from Wolves.

The Brazil midfielder made the third-highest number of tackles in the Premier League last season with 107 and his ball recoveries were the fourth highest in the division - winning possession 194 times.

He adds crucial nous after Villa's plans were thrown a curveball following Amadou Onana's serious knee injury.

Gomes is plug-and-play ready, with 116 top-flight appearances in his three-and-a-half years at Molineux.

Chelsea's Nicolas Jackson could be an option with experience of the league as Villa look for a new striker, with the forward having worked with Emery at Villarreal - but the Blues' £65m price tag will be prohibitive.

Emery knows he cannot afford players to take time to adapt to his meticulous methods so needs men he can trust quickly. AC Milan's former Brighton left-back Pervis Estupinan is therefore a candidate to bolster the defence.

A new winger arrived in the form of Alejandro Garnacho from Chelsea and a right-back who can also play centrally is also on the wishlist.

Last season, Villa's expected points was 48.50, which would have left them 12th - eight places below where they eventually finished.

They were one of the clear over-achievers, with only Sunderland doing better, finishing seventh instead of an expected position of 18th.

Villa did not drop out of the top four after beating Leeds last November, aided by winning 12 of 13 top-flight games between September and December.

Then came the drop off, with just six league wins after the turn of the year as they needed to rely on the failings of others - notably Chelsea - to stay in the top five.

Yet they managed it despite Boubacar Kamara's second-ending injury, John McGinn's knee problem that kept him out for 10 games and the knee issue for Tielemans that sidelined him for two months.

During Tielemans' injury absence between February and April, Villa won just two of eight Premier League games - including a 4-1 defeat to Chelsea that left them on the brink of dropping out of the top four.

Without him, Villa's Premier League win ratio dropped from 53% to 41%.

It all adds to the quality of Emery's work, managing to keep Villa in the top four despite constant setbacks.

Doing it again against a summer of change will be another test of the manager's skill.

title: Russell proud of resilience after string of problems
description: George Russell says he has had to be "the most resilient I've ever been" to cope with the string of problems that have affected his title challenge.
fullText:
George Russell says he has had to be "the most resilient I've ever been" to cope with the string of problems that have affected his title challenge this season.

Russell heads into this weekend's Hungarian Grand Prix 50 points behind Mercedes team-mate Kimi Antonelli after retiring from the Belgian race last weekend following a first-lap collision with Lewis Hamilton.

That doubled his deficit to Antonelli, who won the race to take his sixth victory in 10 grands prix this season.

"I definitely feel this is the most resilient I've ever been in my whole life. I take pride in that as my mentality on the outside of the car," Russell said.

"I guess it's because I still believe in myself and the team, even when everything looks against us. I've been in this position a couple of times this season, and then managed to bounce back and get a good result, or two."

Alonso optimistic about Aston Martin upgrade - will it decide his future?

How to follow Hungarian Grand Prix on the BBC

Russell had reduced his deficit from 66 points to 25 over the three races in Spain, Austria and Britain.

"The run from Monaco to Silverstone, I took strides forward," Russell said. "It was a step back, but I'm still closer today than I was four races ago, and not even halfway through (the season) yet. There's still a lot of things I need to work on myself."

Russell had a particularly trying weekend in Belgium, with a series of engine-related issues affecting his performance.

In the gap between Belgium and Hungary, Mercedes have understood what happened to Russell to leave him short of electrical deployment in the final part of the lap.

This cost him about half of his 0.5-second deficit to Antonelli in qualifying.

And in the race, while both had the same shortage of electrical power on accelerating out of the first corner after the start, circumstances meant Antonelli was less affected by it than Russell.

The Italian lifted into Eau Rouge because he was being challenged by Max Verstappen's Red Bull. That reset his system and meant he had deployment on the following straight.

Russell took Eau Rouge flat, and his lack of deployment left him vulnerable to Hamilton, who collided with him at the following chicane.

He said: "These new power units are just exceptionally complex, and there was just something deeply embedded in the calibration and the code that was almost impossible to see unless you knew exactly what to look for. So I'm glad we have found it.

"Spa and Silverstone probably exposed the issue just because of the nature of the track. Of course it's a bit frustrating to have what happened on Sunday, but I'm just looking forward now, and that's how the game goes."

The knock-on effect of the problem with energy management in Spa was that Russell wasted time chasing it which would have been better devoted to other matters.

"Coming out of Monaco, going into Barcelona and Austria, I made some changes with my driving style, and I felt I made a good step forward," Russell said.

"And obviously I was on pole in both those races, and won in Austria, and just wanted to continue that forward momentum.

"I'm guilty that it has taken probably too much of my focus, and I'm spending a lot of time trying to change my driving style again, more for the power unit, rather than just trying to drive fast, so, again, it's learning.

"We recognised after Spa, it wasn't in the driving style. So that is something, at least, that is a learning that has been validated that I didn't know pre-Spa."

Hungary is a circuit entirely different in character to Spa and Silverstone and might be expected to favour Ferrari because of their strong cornering performance.

But both Lewis Hamilton and Charles Leclerc played down their chances, even though they have won two of the past four races.

Hamilton said: "Mercedes are still the team to beat. They've won almost all the races, they're quick in all circuits, fast in slow-speed circuits, the quickest in Monaco. You have to anticipate they could be quickest this weekend as well."

McLaren have a major upgrade for this weekend that the team hope will move them back towards the front, but Lando Norris was also cautious about his prospects.

"We want to be optimistic about it," the world champion said. "We want to be confident and hope that it fixes some of our issues that we're currently struggling with and limiting us with.

"There's been a few upgrades this year that have maybe not worked exactly as we would have hoped, so I think we're just being a little bit more cautious on waiting to see if it works exactly as we expect."

Why was Hamilton penalised but not Leclerc? - F1 Q&A

title: Carapaz wins stage as Pogacar's team suffers sickness
description: Ecuador's Richard Carapaz wins stage 18 of the Tour de France following a commanding breakaway.
fullText:
Ecuador's Richard Carapaz won stage 18 of the Tour de France following a commanding breakaway.

The EF Education-Easypost rider brilliantly accelerated away from his rivals with intuitive and canny riding, finishing 45 seconds ahead of Switzerland's Mauro Schmid of Jayco-Alula and Matteo Jorgenson of Visma-Lease a Bike.

Meanwhile, sickness appears to be making its way through overall leader Tadej Pogacar's UAE Team Emirates-XRG squad after key domestique Brandon McNulty abandoned the race mid-stage.

Slovenian Pogacar remains in the yellow jersey by four minutes 32 seconds over Belgium's Remco Evenepeol of Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe and six minutes 51 seconds over UAE team-mate Isaac del Toro of Mexico.

Thursday's mountainous 185.2km stage from Voiron to Ocieres Merlette marked the first of three gruelling days in the French Alps, and several riders formed a number of breakaways on a hot day on the road.

But Carapaz's combative riding was the best to watch. With his gold chain and giant crucifix swinging from side to side, he danced on the pedals to try to launch himself away from the hard-charging Valentin Paret-Peintre of Soudal Quick Step - who is wearing the King of the Mountains jersey on behalf of KOM leader Pogacar.

When Paret-Peintre avoided helping the pair extend their lead over Jorgenson and Schmid, Carapaz virtually stopped in the steep tarmac, baulking Paret-Peintre, and as the Frenchman then assumed a period of recovery, Carapaz launched again, leaving an exhausted and out-of-rhythm Paret-Peintre broken.

Denmark's Mads Pedersen extended his lead in the green jersey classification to 32 points, but remains vulnerable to Belgium's Jasper Philipsen.

Pogacar, 27, retains the King of the Mountain jersey, but only by a point, as does Del Toro in the white young rider's jersey.

Dominant overall leader Pogacar is in a commanding position in the general classification as the race heads into its conclusion in the Alps.

Pogacar is aiming to become the joint-most successful rider in the Tour's history with five wins, alongside Jacques Anquetil, Eddy Merckx, Bernard Hinault and Miguel Indurain.

But the loss of American rider McNulty to sickness - after he drifted several minutes behind the peloton early in the stage - follows Britain's Adam Yates falling ill and vomiting after stage 16's time trial.

Yates spent the following stage riding slowly at the very back of the field, and finished almost last, more than 26 minutes down.

On Thursday Yates was again forced to drift back to his team's service car and take on fluids, before eventually finishing nearly half an hour down.

Such is Pogacar's dominance of the sport, losing two domestiques might not seen like such a problem.

But he uses his domestiques early on stages which involve a lot of climbing, and the next two stages which both finish on the iconic ribbon of Tarmac up to the Alpe d'Huez ski resort, promise to be particularly brutal.

Could even Pogacar's phenomenal pace and explosive riding be quelled by having to traverse a total of four uncategorised climbs?

title: Newcastle and Liverpool planning Keegan tribute at season opener
description: Newcastle United and Liverpool are in discussions over a tribute to Kevin Keegan before their Premier League opener at St James' Park on Sunday 23rd August.
fullText:
Newcastle United and Liverpool are in discussions over a tribute to Kevin Keegan before their Premier League opener at St James' Park.

Keegan, who died at the age of 75 this week, is revered at both clubs, having enjoyed legendary spells as a player with Liverpool and as both player and manager at Newcastle.

The two clubs are to face each other in the opening match of their 2026-27 season on Sunday, 23 August (16:30 BST).

Newcastle said in a statement that they are talking to Liverpool "to ensure [Keegan's] memory is marked fittingly before the game".

The club added they "will work closely with supporter groups... as well as the Premier League, to shape tributes".

Keegan spent two seasons as a Newcastle player from 1982 before returning as their manager in 1992 for the first of two spells in charge. He won three league titles, the European Cup, two Uefa Cups and the FA Cup during a trophy-laden six years at Liverpool.

Gifted, charismatic and passionate amid highs and lows - Keegan obituary

Newcastle's men's, women's and under-21 sides will wear black armbands during their pre-season matches to honour Keegan, who guided the club to Premier League promotion in 1992-93 and second place in the 1995-96 top flight.

A minute's silence will be observed before Eddie Howe's side play Gateshead in an away friendly this Saturday.

Keegan's photo will be displayed on screens at St James' Park in the seventh minute - a reference to the number seven shirt he wore as a player - when the men's side face Bayer Leverkusen and Strasbourg on 15 and 16 August.

The same tribute will take place when the women's team face Inter Milan on 16 August, giving supporters the chance to join a minute's applause for the former England captain and manager.

Newcastle said they will consult Keegan's family on plans for a permanent tribute.

In June, Keegan had revealed he had stage four cancer, which is the most advanced stage of cancer and means the disease has spread to other parts of the body.

His family initially disclosed he was battling the disease in January.

A statement on behalf of the Keegan family this week said he was "surrounded by his wife and daughters in his final moments".

During an illustrious playing career, he also enjoyed spells at Scunthorpe United, Hamburg and Southampton before managing Newcastle, Fulham, England and Manchester City.

Ferguson says Keegan built Newcastle's greatest team

'I'm so honoured to have known him' - Shearer on Keegan

title: Fatal police shooting of man officials say was armed with knife prompts protests in city with troubled use-of-force history
description: A man was shot and killed in a struggle with police officers Wednesday in Madison, Wisconsin, after officials say he pulled out a knife and injured an officer – a confrontation that has sparked outcry...
fullText:
A man was shot and killed in a struggle with police officers Wednesday in Madison, Wisconsin, after officials say he pulled out a knife and injured an officer – a confrontation that has sparked outcry from protesters in a city that has previously been rocked by anger over police-involved shootings.

The man killed was in his 30s, according to Madison Police Chief John Patterson, and his name has not been released. The Wisconsin Department of Justice's Division of Criminal Investigation is reviewing the shooting, which happened less than two miles from the state Capitol.

"There must be transparency and accountability in any investigation moving forward," Democratic Gov. Tony Evers posted to Facebook. "We will continue to keep the loved ones and family of the individual who was killed in our hearts and prayers. His life mattered."

Like many deadly police-involved shootings in the past, questions around the shooting center on how much of a threat the officer faced, the lack of body-worn cameras and what can be seen in a bystander video.

Police encountered the man while responding to a report of a person checking parked vehicles Wednesday afternoon, Patterson said during a news conference Wednesday.

On Thursday, a spokesperson for the police department said officers "were called to the area for someone stealing bicycles and checking vehicle."

Officers tried to make contact with him, but he fled on his bicycle through nearby backyards, according to police.

When they caught up to him later near the intersection of Williamson and South Baldwin streets on the city's Near East Side, "a physical struggle ensued," Patterson said. The man pulled out a fixed-blade knife and injured one officer, described as a "veteran officer," who was trying to arrest him, the chief added.

"This was not a small, folding knife," Patterson said. "It's a large, fixed-blade knife."

Bystander video filmed from a nearby car shows officers on the ground at an intersection, struggling with a man who is then pulled up to his feet. One officer can be heard yelling, "He's got a knife," while another yells "Taser! Taser! Taser," deploying it as a police cruiser quickly pulls up. As the man begins to fall back to the ground and has his legs pinned by an officer's foot, another officer appears to pull a gun, and three shots are heard. The officer who had drawn the Taser holds her hand to her mouth as she backs away.

It is not clear what happened before the video began and authorities are investigating whether the man fell from his bicycle or was physically removed by officers, Patterson said.

"I encourage people to watch the video," Michael Doubek, a witness who recorded it, told CNN affiliate WISC, saying he saw the man shot in the head. "I think it's a pretty horrifying look at what happened."

Sabrina Madison, president of Madison's city council, told CNN she was shocked to see in the video that less than three seconds passed between the Taser deployment and the fatal gunshots.

"I just cannot let go that there are four officers, and there are three seconds where the officer at close range shoots him in the head three times," she said. "That is heartbreaking."

Patterson said he had seen the bystander video, saying it "is a perspective of what occurred."

"I caution everyone, this is one perspective. It is not a full overview of everything that was visible from different angles, certainly visible from the officers," he said.

The Wisconsin Professional Police Association called for patience.

"An independent investigation is underway, and we believe conclusions about what occurred should be based on the totality of the evidence, not incomplete information or any single recording that may capture only one perspective," WPPA executive director Jim Palmer said in a statement to CNN.

The top prosecutor in Madison declined to comment on the case Thursday.

"I am ethically bound to not speak about open investigations and/or potential prosecutions," Dane County District Attorney Ismael Ozanne told CNN in an email.

A second officer was injured in the incident, though Patterson said it's unclear how the injury occurred. He added authorities don't believe the injury was caused by the man's knife.

None of the officers involved was wearing a body camera, according to the police chief, adding the department does not have "any broad deployment of body cameras in Madison, unfortunately."

Last month, Patterson publicly called for city funding for body-worn cameras, CNN affiliate WKOW reported, and Madison told CNN Thursday she expects that budget request to be approved. But even with funding, the plan calls for the cameras to be phased in over three years.

"Regrettably, because Madison officers are not equipped with body-worn cameras, investigators and the public will not have the benefit of video from the officers' perspectives and will instead have to rely on other available video and evidence to help establish the full circumstances of what occurred," Palmer said.

The Division of Criminal Investigation will review all footage posted online or submitted directly to the agency to assess the situation.

All four officers involved have been placed on administrative leave pending the investigation, Patterson said.

CNN reached out to the Madison Police Department for additional information and was referred back to an incident report.

Patterson acknowledged the shooting "occurred in a neighborhood that has experienced a lot of trauma in the past."

On the same street in 2015, a White police officer shot and killed an unarmed, biracial 19-year-old, Tony Robinson, leading to major protests. Three years before that, Paul Heenan, a 30-year-old White musician, was shot to death after mistakenly entering the wrong house and scuffling with police who responded.

In both cases, the shooting officer's actions were found to be justified. The city paid Robinson's and Heenan's families a total of $5.65 million in settlements, Robinson's attorneys said.

The race of the victim and the officer from Wednesday's shooting have not been disclosed.

Black community and social activist groups have condemned the latest killing, as peaceful protesters gathered Wednesday in Madison, chanting "No justice, no peace." By Thursday morning, protesters were camping at the intersection where the shooting took place, blocking it with a sofa and mattresses. Candles, flowers and protest signs were placed at the scene.

"I understand the anger, grief, and fear people are feeling — because I am feeling that too. As a city, as a community, as a society, we need to do better," Madison Mayor Satya Rhodes-Conway wrote in a statement expressing her condolences. "Any officer-involved shooting needs to be rigorously investigated, and this one will be. While we do not yet know all the details, our community deserves full transparency and accountability."

This story has been updated with additional information.

CNN's Jillian Sykes, Amanda Jackson, Jennifer Feldman, Kara Devlin and Chuck Johnston contributed to this report.

title: Nun who was detained by ICE while walking to Mass in Texas says many are in her situation
description: A nun who was taken into immigration custody after being arrested while walking to Mass in her habit last month in Texas pleaded Thursday for people to have the chance to be heard before being taken i...
fullText:
McALLEN, Texas (AP) — A nun who was taken into immigration custody after being arrested while walking to Mass in her habit last month in Texas pleaded Thursday for people to have the chance to be heard before being taken into custody, saying she was speaking out to help others.

"There are many others in this same situation, people who have been given some form of protection under our laws, who have followed every rule asked of them, and who still live with uncertainty," Sister Leticia Ugboaja, who is from Nigeria, said at a news conference.

Ugboaja was released several hours after being detained by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers on June 28 while walking to Our Lady of Sorrows Church in McAllen, a few miles from the U.S.-Mexico border.

Parish officials posting on social media brought attention to Ugboaja's arrest, and Congress members ultimately intervened on the nun's behalf.

Her attorney, Carlos Garcia, said Thursday that in 2019 an immigration judge denied her application for asylum but also granted her protection from removal because she was likely to face torture in Nigeria. Garcia said Ugboaja has complied with everything required of her and had permission to work in the U.S.

When ICE detained Ugboaja in June, she wasn't given much explanation as to why she was taken into custody but that the U.S. planned to remove her to a third country, Garcia said.

Lawyers and human rights groups have criticized the Trump administration's third-country removal policy, which has faced legal challenges.

The Department of Homeland Security and ICE did not immediately respond to a request to comment on Ugboaja's case on Thursday and did not respond to requests for comment after her detention in June.

Ugboaja has a check-in with ICE set for next week, her lawyer noted.

Ugboaja is part of the Daughters of Mary Mother of Mercy and volunteered as an Extraordinary Minister of Holy Communion at Our Lady of Sorrows Church, a spokesperson for the Diocese of Brownsville has said. Ugboaja is also a registered nurse at South Texas Health System and worked previously for 10 years as a certified nursing assistant in Edinburg, the spokesperson said.

President Donald Trump's immigration crackdown — including at sensitive sites such as houses of worship — has prompted faith leaders to adjust their response to church members who have become too afraid to attend. Some have encouraged online attendance, while others have offered help doing errands such as grocery shopping for people too fearful to leave their homes.

title: Super Giants thrash dismal Spirit at Lord's
description: Manchester Super Giants chase a target of 81 inside 59 balls as they thrash London Spirit by seven wickets in The Hundred.
fullText:
The Hundred women's competition, Lord's

London Spirit 80 all out (91 balls): Kapp 17 (12); MacDonald-Gay 4-17

Manchester Super Giants 86-3 (59 balls): Scholfield 22 (15); Kapp 2-18

Manchester Super Giants won by seven wickets

Manchester Super Giants chased a target of 81 in just 59 balls as they thrashed London Spirit by seven wickets in The Hundred.

Ryana MacDonald-Gay took 4-17 as Super Giants bowled out Spirit with nine balls to spare at Lord's, before the visiting batters completed their chase for the loss of just three wickets.

Spirit were in immediate trouble after being putting in to bat in their opening fixture, losing openers Grace Harris and Amy Jones for four and a golden duck.

MacDonald-Gay removed Marie Kelly and Marizanne Kapp, who top-scored with 17, while Deandra Dottin was bowled by Sophie Ecclestone for four.

Home captain Charlie Dean was one of only four batters to make it into double figures, reaching 14 before she was run out, with the innings coming to an early conclusion as MacDonald-Gay had Katie George caught behind for eight.

Super Giants, playing their first match under their new name having spent five years as Manchester Originals, made steady work chasing their low target.

Smriti Mandhana and captain Meg Lanning put on 33 for the opening wicket, before the former was trapped lbw by Kapp for 17.

Kapp struck with the next delivery, bowling Grace Scrivens, but Paige Scholfield survived the hat-trick ball.

Lanning was caught off Hannah Rainey for 21, but Richa Ghosh (15 not out off five) and Scholfield (22 off 15) swiftly finished the job, with the latter hitting a back-to-back four and six to win the game.

Who am I? Guess The Hundred cricket star No 3

The Hundred 2026 - schedule and how to follow

Birmingham Phoenix will take on Trent Rockets at Edgbaston on Friday, with the women's game starting at 15:00 BST and the men's at 18:30.

You can follow ball-by-ball commentary on BBC Radio 5 Sports Extra, BBC Sounds and the BBC Sport website and app.

title: Garnacho joins Villa on loan from Chelsea
description: Aston Villa sign Chelsea winger Alejandro Garnacho in a loan deal that will become permanent "if certain criteria are met".
fullText:
Aston Villa have announced the loan signing of Chelsea winger Alejandro Garnacho until the end of the 2026-27 season.

The 22-year-old's transfer will "become permanent next summer should certain criteria be met", said the Blues, with the Argentine already agreeing a four-year contract at Villa Park.

The terms of the obligation to buy clause have not been disclosed by either club, but it is expected to be triggered if conditions, such as the number of appearances Garnacho makes next season, are met.

Chelsea believe the terms are easily reachable and they will achieve their original £43m valuation.

Garnacho's departure from Stamford Bridge follows Chelsea's signing of Morgan Rogers for a record £117m fee from Villa on Tuesday.

Villa head coach Unai Emery said he was "delighted" with Garnacho's arrival.

"He is so talented, young and he showed us his wish to help our project. We are really happy," he added.

The winger becomes Villa's fourth signing, after the arrivals of Johan Manzambi, Joao Gomes and Modou Keba Cisse.

Villa's player sales this summer, meanwhile, total nearly £200m, with Rogers, Youri Tielemans, Donyell Malen, Enzo Barrenechea and Lewis Dobbin all leaving.

The Blues have been keen to offload Garnacho despite signing him only last September in a £40m deal from Manchester United, who would be due 10% of any eventual transfer fee.

His impact at Stamford Bridge has been limited to one Premier League goal, and the arrivals of Rogers and Sporting winger Geovany Quenda have increased the need to reduce the size of the squad.

Garnacho, Rogers and two clubs trying to balance the books

Garnacho arrives at Aston Villa still with more promise than pedigree.

Emery has shown faith in moving for the eight-cap Argentina international as he looks to rebuild his squad at Villa Park.

With Rogers moving to Stamford Bridge for a club-record sale of £117m, the club needed to act.

They have been fluid in the market, an important stance given their finances and the general nature of the window, and Garnacho - who also interested Roma - represented a good opportunity.

Emery wanted a pacy winger and, with the option of an initial loan with an obligation to buy if certain clauses are met, the deal also gave Villa a degree of control over finances given they must still tread lightly with the Premier League's and Uefa's financial fair play rules.

Should the move become permanent - and Garnacho has already agreed a four-year contract - then he will have achieved something other attackers have not when on loan at Villa.

Marcus Rashford, Jadon Sancho and Harvey Elliott joined previously with varying degrees of success in their temporary moves.

Rashford's 17-game spell at Villa Park in 2024-25 led to an England return, while Sancho flattered to deceive and Emery left Elliott in limbo with his decision not to play him more, to avoid triggering an obligation for a permanent transfer.

Clearly surplus to requirements at Chelsea, Garnacho does not want to return to somewhere he is not needed.

A return of one goal and four assists in 24 Premier League games for the Blues last season - or eight goals in 43 appearances in all competitions - suggests he will need to raise his game to meet the level Emery demands.

Eight assists and 16 goals in 93 games at Manchester United also shows Garnacho's end product must improve at Villa.

There is clearly talent there and it is now up to him to show it, and for Emery to harness it.

title: Kozo Okamoto, Japanese militant who took part in a 1972 Tel Aviv airport attack, dies in Beirut
description: Kozo Okamoto, the Japanese militant who took part in a deadly attack on Tel Aviv’s airport more than five decades ago, died Thursday in Beirut, the Palestinian group that had been protecting him said....
fullText:
BEIRUT (AP) — Kozo Okamoto, the Japanese militant who took part in a deadly attack on Tel Aviv's airport more than five decades ago, died Thursday in Beirut, the Palestinian group that had been protecting him said. He was 78.

The Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine, a faction that is the second-largest group in the Palestine Liberation Organization, said that Okamoto had been ill for a long time.

Okamoto, who made rare public appearances since he settled in Lebanon after being released from Israel, was known within the PFLP as Ahmad al-Yabani, or Ahmad the Japanese.

The PFLP said in its statement that Okamoto's journey was "marked by immense sacrifices spanning for decades on the battlefields of struggle in support of the Palestinian cause." The Palestinian group added that Okamoto "never compromised nor faltered but remained steadfast to his principles."

Okamoto had served 12 years in an Israeli lockup for a May 30, 1972 attack on the international airport outside Tel Aviv that was thought to be carried out by members of the Japanese Red Army guerrilla group in a joint operation with the PFLP.

In the 1972 attack, Okamoto and two of his colleagues arrived in Tel Aviv on a flight from Europe, then collected their bags, in which they had packed rifles and grenades, and opened fire, killing 26 people and wounding dozens, according to AP reports.

The two Japanese with Okamoto were killed in the attack while he was wounded.

Okamoto was sentenced to life in prison but freed in 1985 in a prisoner exchange between Israel and Palestinian guerrillas. He later gained political asylum in Lebanon, where he lived until his death.

One of the rare public appearances that Okamoto made was in May 2022 at a Beirut cemetery, where Palestinian militants and other personalities are buried, to mark the 50th anniversary of the attack.

The Japanese Red Army, a violent ultra-leftist group that had links to Palestinian militants, was formed in 1971 and took responsibility for several international attacks, including the takeover of the U.S. Consulate in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, in 1975.

The PFLP is a radical faction of the Palestine Liberation Organization and gained notoriety after the simultaneous hijackings of four Western airliners in 1970 and the seizure of an Air France flight en route from Tel Aviv to Paris that was diverted to Entebbe, Uganda.

The PFLP said in a statement Thursday that Okamoto's attack at the Israeli airport was in retaliation for a 1968 Israeli commando attack on Beirut's international airport.

The leader of the PFLP, Ahmed Saadat, is one of the most prominent Palestinians held in Israeli jails. Saadat was accused of organizing the 2001 assassination of Israeli Tourism Minister Rehavam Zeevi, an ultranationalist who called for the mass expulsion of Palestinians.

Okamoto and four other Japanese were arrested by Lebanese authorities in 1997 in eastern Lebanon after spending years illegally in the country, protected by Palestinian and Lebanese leftist groups during the chaotic days of the 1975-90 civil war. They were put on trial and the four were handed over to Japan in 2000 while Okamoto became the first person to get political asylum in Lebanon.

Japan has been demanding for years that Okamoto be handed over by Lebanon, but Beirut has repeatedly rejected the demand. He is considered a hero by many in Lebanon and the Arab world for championing the Palestinian cause and opposing Israel.

Okamoto once described himself during his trial in Lebanon as "an Arab resistance fighter."

title: Arsenal set to make £70m bid for Newcastle's Guimaraes
description: Arsenal are set to make a £70m offer for Newcastle captain Bruno Guimaraes.
fullText:
Arsenal are set to make a £70m offer for Newcastle captain Bruno Guimaraes.

Brazil international Guimaraes is a priority target for the Gunners, with the midfielder keen to join the Premier League champions.

Newcastle are aware of the 28-year-old's preference to join Arsenal this summer.

Talks over a potential move for Guimaraes are ongoing and are believed to have accelerated in recent days.

The agreement of personal terms is not expected to be an issue, though it remains to be seen if Arsenal's £70m offer will meet Newcastle's valuation.

Sources close to Newcastle insist the Magpies have no intention of selling Guimaraes this summer and that there has been no direct approach from the Gunners for their player.

Arsenal are determined to strengthen their squad with high quality players this summer as they look to build on last season's title.

The north London side were interested in signing Morgan Rogers, who has joined Chelsea for £117m, from Aston Villa – but refused to go higher than their £80m valuation of the player.

It is understood Arsenal were given the opportunity to match Chelsea's offer for Rogers but declined.

Similarly, Arsenal have a hardline maximum valuation of Guimaraes and are unwilling to overpay despite their desire to sign the midfielder.

Arsenal also have a major interest in Villa defender Ezri Konsa and have enquired about the England international's availability.

Konsa is Arsenal's first-choice defensive target.

The Gunners also have Como's Jacobo Ramon on a list of defenders they would like to bring in, but it is understood clauses in the Spaniard's contract make a deal difficult.

Having missed out on Rogers, the Gunners remain in the market for attacking reinforcements.

Atletico Madrid forward Julian Alvarez is Arsenal's dream target, but the player's current desire to join Barcelona and the cost - which could exceed the British record fee of £125m set when Alexander Isak moved from Newcastle to Liverpool - pose obvious difficulties.

Arsenal have also explored a move for Paris St-Germain winger Bradley Barcola.

title: The Scotland-England sister rivalry at Glasgow 2026
description: Teenage sisters Astrid and Ursula Carroll are set to swim against each other at the Commonwealth Games - but for different countries.
fullText:
Teenage sisters Astrid and Ursula Carroll will both compete at the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow - but for different countries.

Astrid, 17, was born in Edinburgh and swims for Scotland, but 14-year-old Ursula was born in Halifax and represents England.

The visually impaired siblings will line up against each other in the women's 100m freestyle S13 heats on Friday morning.

"It'll be nice walking out and just seeing you," laughs Astrid.

Ursula smiles before responding: "Scottish, unbelievable."

Sat around the kitchen table at their family cottage in Holmfirth, Yorkshire, they joke between themselves - but the atmosphere won't necessarily be this friendly once the competition starts.

"I've swam with you almost most of my life anyway, but it will be nice to be competing against you," adds Ursula, who is the youngest member of England's team at the Games.

The teenagers train together at Stockport Metro Swimming Club, but it will be the first time they have raced each other in competition, presenting some unusual problems for their family.

"I think our grandparents were talking about wearing a T-shirt with the Scottish flag on the front and the English flag on the back or something," says Astrid.

Ursula smirks as she replies: "Why is the English on the back?"

Astrid normally competes in the S12 category, which is lower on the scale for visually impaired swimmers, while Ursula is in the S13 category.

But under Commonwealth rules all visually impaired athletes swim together in one category, setting up a debut competitive meet between them.

They will compete in freestyle races, rather than the breaststroke or backstroke events that Astrid won medals in at last year's Para-swimming World Championships in Singapore.

"Sadly, Ursula is better at front crawl, so I might have to settle," admits Astrid.

Ursula laughs as she responds: "This is news to me!"

The sisters have Stargardt disease - a rare, inherited eye condition that results in a progressive loss of central vision, where a build-up of unprocessed vitamin A at the back of the eye in the retina causes it to tear over time.

"It's basically like seeing through a doughnut or like bubble wrap," explains Astrid.

"Over Covid it got a lot worse, so that was quite difficult going back to the pool and adjusting after that."

Working alongside her coaches, Astrid has adapted by incorporating specialised techniques to enhance her swimming performance.

"I do a lot of straight counting," she says. "So I will count the amount of strokes and then know that amount and then turn.

"I'm also introducing tappers at the moment, it's basically like you'll have a person on the side that will hit you on the head when you are a certain amount of strokes out and that's how you know when to turn.

"It's just adapting the coaching techniques to being less visual and more getting very good at verbally describing what you want from the technique."

Ursula, who was also diagnosed with the degenerative eye condition at eight years old, says her elder sister has been a huge inspiration.

"I think you're quite a motivated person," she tells Astrid.

'It's really, really helpful just being around you generally because it just makes me push myself as much as you push yourself.

"Especially since you've been in the sport for quite a long time, I can almost use you as a resource to help me through the sport and help me understand everything about it."

Astrid brushes off Ursula's compliments.

"We'll see about that later," she replies.

"No, it's quite nice when everything else feels quite daunting or new or special. Just being like, this is someone that's reliable, it's just really nice."

The teenagers will make their international debuts for Scotland and England in Glasgow.

"It's an amazing opportunity," admits Astrid.

"I think both of us are really looking forward to getting in and racing and being able to turn that non-competitive sibling thing into 'let's get into the pool and race a good race!'."

With another smile, Ursula adds: "It's going to be really interesting going against you. We'll just have to see about it [the result]."

The sisters break into laughter as Astrid says: "You're a competitive enough person, I'm sure we can make it a nice competition.

"You should just have joined Scotland!"

title: JJ Gabriel, 15, gets senior Man Utd call-up
description: Manchester United teenager JJ Gabriel, 15, could make senior debut on Friday after receiving call-up for Rosenborg friendly.
fullText:
Fifteen-year-old forward JJ Gabriel has been named in Manchester United's senior squad for the first time for Friday's friendly against Rosenborg.

The youngster, who won both the Premier League and United's Under-18 awards last season despite only being 14 when the campaign began, is part of Michael Carrick's 25-man travelling party for the pre-season friendly in Trondheim.

Gabriel, who doesn't turn 16 until October, is the only change to the squad that was beaten 1-0 by Wrexham in Finland last week with striker Ethan Wheatley dropping out.

It means there is no place for 18-year-old Tynan Thompson, who has joined from Tottenham in an £8m deal. Striker Benjamin Sesko, goalkeeper Karl Darlow and defender Matthijs de Ligt are all ruled out due to injury.

It had been expected Gabriel would play some part in United's pre-season this summer given the progress the Londoner has made through the club's youth system.

But should the winger feature in Norway to make his first senior appearance for the club, it will be the latest milestone in the teenager's career.

Manchester United travelling squad: Tom Heaton, Radek Vitek, Harry Maguire, Leny Yoro, Luke Shaw, Ayden Heaven, Mason Mount, Joshua Zirkzee, Patrick Chinazaekpere Dorgu, , Andrey Santos, Bryan Mbeumo, Chido Obi, Jack Fletcher, Tyler Fletcher, Harry Amass, Toby Collyer, Dan Gore, Dermot Mee, Shea Lacey, Ethan Williams, Jaydan Kamason, Jacob Devaney, Jim Thwaites, Dan Armer, JJ Gabriel.

One of the most talked about young players in England's academy system, director of football Jason Wilcox was personally involved in persuading Gabriel to remain at United last summer.

It is part of a concerted effort to bring promising young talents to the Old Trafford club, which Thompson's arrival from Tottenham is part of.

Gabriel first came to notice outside United circles in April 2025.

Striker Victor Musa scored six times as United beat Leeds 13-1 in a lopsided Premier Under 18 League encounter.

But neither the score, nor Musa's double hat-trick was the main talking point that day – it was a 25-minute cameo from Gabriel on his debut, then aged just 14 and a half.

Pace, balance and control were already evident. He also scored twice to show he has an eye for goal.

He built on that early promise last season. For most of the campaign he seemed destined to finish as the league's top scorer, only for Manchester City's Teddie Lamb to pip him in the final couple of games.

Gabriel was a stand-out performer last term, scoring 29 times in 32 appearances for the Red Devils, helping the them to reach the FA Youth Cup and Premier League Under-18 cup finals.

He was initially called up for first-team training by Ruben Amorim and has been a regular since, with Carrick keeping a close eye on United's academy teams.

Gabriel was not involved in last week's defeat by Wrexham in Helsinki as he had returned to training less than a week earlier, but United are carefully handling a precocious talent.

The teenager was invited into the directors' box on a number of occasions last season and met with Sir Alex Ferguson following a draw against West Ham at Old Trafford in December.

Such access is not commonplace, which underlines how much promise United see in the youngster.

For context, Gabriel is just about to enter his GCSE year. He played – and scored - in last season's FA Youth Cup at a younger age than legendary Manchester United figures such as Duncan Edwards and Eddie Colman from the Busby Babes and also Ryan Giggs.

Of course, there have been plenty of talented young players – Arsenal's Max Dowman is a current example. Not all fulfil their potential.

What offers additional hope around Gabriel is his size.

He has made such a significant impact despite his slight frame. United's coaching staff moved him inside from his preferred position cutting in from the left last season to keep him away from full backs who were bigger and stronger and could try and muscle him out of the way.

There was a belief Gabriel would grow this summer and judging by his social media feed, he has been working on that.

Having decided to target the youth market to offer the best chance of future success, it would be counterproductive to hold back someone already in their ranks, so Gabriel's call-up for Norway makes perfect sense.

If Gabriel features in Norway he will be one of the youngest players ever to play a senior game. The youngest player to feature in a competitive match for United is David Gaskell, a goalkeeper who made his debut when he was 16 years and 19 days. Gabriel will not be 16 until 6 October.

Not everyone in Carrick's squad is guaranteed to play.

United took 25 men to Helsinki for the defeat by Wrexham. Even though Carrick picked completely different teams for each half, goalkeeper Dermot Mee, midfielder Jim Thwaites and striker Chido Obi were not used.

But if he gets a chance, it will be instructive for United's coaches to see how he copes with senior opponents. That will determine Gabriel's future games programme as United's World Cup players begin to return.

title: Top Democrat erupts at Waltz over troop deaths in Iran: ‘You should resign’
description: Rep. Ted Lieu (D-Calif.) on Wednesday erupted at United Nations Ambassador Mike Waltz over the deaths of U.S. service members in Iran, urging that he “should resign.
fullText:
Rep. Ted Lieu (D-Calif.) on Wednesday erupted at United Nations Ambassador Mike Waltz over the deaths of U.S. service members in Iran, urging that he "should resign."

Waltz was testifying before the House Committee on Foreign Affairs. Lieu asked the ambassador questions about troops based in Jordan, where recent Iranian attacks on U.S. bases killed three service members, before blasting him over the conflict.

"You should be shameful," Lieu yelled. "You don't know basic facts. You should resign. You don't even care about the number of U.S. troops that were wounded. You did not know how many US troops were wounded. You gave false information to Congress. You should be ashamed of yourself for not even knowing how many U.S. troops were wounded in the Iranian war."

Lieu said U.S. troops in Jordan were "exposed" to the threat of Iranian missile and drone strikes.

Rep. Chris Smith (R-N.J.) interjected, urging Lieu to not use "such derogatory language" toward the U.N. ambassador. Rep. Joe Wilson (R-S.C.) added that Waltz was a 27-year military veteran

"Seriously, I know you're upset, but you only bring dishonor to yourself," Smith told Lieu, later adding, "Disagree all you want, but do it civilly, and that was not civil."

Tensions have grown among lawmakers fuming over the Iran war, which has dragged out for nearly five months. Rep. Rich McCormick (R-Ga.) on Monday said the U.S. should have finished the job in Iran "a long time ago."

President Trump said early in the conflict that it would be an "excursion" and end within days or weeks. The U.S. and Iran had an interim peace deal in place, but that deal was shredded after hostilities reignited last week.

Since the conflict resumed, four more U.S. service members have died, including the three killed at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan on Friday and a fourth killed on Sunday during the controlled detonation of a downed Iranian attack drone at Erbil Air Base in Iraq.

Trump attended the dignified transfer of the four service members at Dover Air Force Base on Wednesday. The bodies of 1st Lt. Tyler J. Feehan, Sgt. Michael E. Swinton, Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales were received by Trump and their families.

The four deaths raised the American death toll to 17. The Defense Department has stated that nearly 100 troops have been injured since strikes resumed on July 7, though 96 percent of those troops have returned to duty.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, who lawmakers grilled on Tuesday over the war, said the U.S. has spent $37.5 billion to fight the conflict and requested a $67 billion package to replenish stockpiles that have been used up during the conflict and advance other Trump administration priorities.

title: NATO is looking to create the same battlefield feedback loop Ukraine has
description: In Ukraine, data from the front is relayed to the defense industry, which can quickly field new tech before Russia adapts and it becomes obsolete.
fullText:
NATO wants to establish the same kind of battlefield feedback loop that Ukraine has set up.

In Ukraine, data from the front lines is relayed to industry, which quickly develops new tech.

It is then deployed, often within weeks, before Russia can adapt and render it obsolete.

NATO leadership is looking for ways to mirror Ukraine's battlefield feedback loop, in which issues from the front lines are relayed to the defense industry, which quickly fields solutions for soldiers.

US Air Force Gen. Alexus Grynkewich, who oversees NATO military operations as the Supreme Allied Commander Europe, said Ukraine has built high-level contacts with the defense industry "that allow them to innovate very quickly."

Grynkewich, one of NATO's two strategic commanders, told Business Insider at the Farnborough International Airshow in England this week that some allied countries are already involved in these efforts.

For instance, NATO's Forward Land Forces Battlegroup in Romania, led by France, is buying commercial off-the-shelf technology from trusted vendors and immediately testing it in the field.

"So we've got places where we're learning," Grynkewich said. "That's a whole structural thing that'll take some time to adapt across the entire alliance, but we know where we need to go."

Grynkewich said one of the reasons thay he traveled to Farnborough, which showcases the latest in defense technology, was to build what he described as "connective tissue at the senior level."

Ukrainian officials have likened the arms race between Ukraine and Russia to a cat-and-mouse game. In Kyiv, drones, robots, and other new technologies move from the design phase to field testing and, finally, into the hands of soldiers within weeks or months.

Battlefield feedback is quickly shared with arms makers so that they can develop solutions to pressing issues on the front lines. The goal for Ukraine is to innovate faster than Russia can adapt, especially in new drone capabilities, so the product doesn't become obsolete.

NATO leadership has long praised Ukraine's ability to quickly react to the changing battlefield. Some Western officials have said they want the alliance to demonstrate a similar sense of urgency for weapons development and procurement.

NATO isn't driven by the same wartime pressures as Ukraine, but officials and security experts argue that the alliance can't afford to sit back as it prepares for a potential future confrontation with Russia.

The development cycle for complex systems built by legacy defense contractors for past wars — warships, aircraft, and armored vehicles — can take years. However, newer Western startups breaking into the field are trying to more closely emulate the Ukrainian model.

Some war technologies have a quicker innovation cycle than others.

"All the software-centric capabilities are easy to update," said French Adm. Pierre Vandier, NATO's Supreme Allied Commander Transformation.

"And so I think we are transitioning from complex-to-update to easy-to-update platforms, intrinsically. That means that the design is now fitted for rapid adaptation," Vandier, NATO's other strategic commander, told Business Insider in an interview this week.

"This is the good news, and I think everybody's going on that. Some are quicker than the others, but it's the global trend," he said.

Because NATO isn't in an active conflict, though, it is hamstrung by regulations and laws that could slow development and innovation.

"I think to match that, we need to have structures that are ready to shift," Vandier said, adding that when the constraints are ultimately lifted, the alliance will be "ready to accelerate."

If you enjoyed this story, be sure to follow Business Insider on Yahoo.

title: $290M cocaine haul found ‘skillfully concealed’ in massive banana shipment
description: Italian officials said the 1,700-pound haul of cocaine, hidden among banana pallets, could have generated roughly $290 million on the street.
fullText:
Italian authorities seized roughly 1,700 pounds of cocaine hidden among pallets of bananas at a major port, officials said Tuesday.

The drugs were divided into 650 packages and "skillfully concealed" inside a refrigerated shipping container that arrived at the port of Vado Ligure from one of Ecuador's main ports, according to Italy's Guardia di Finanza.

A field test conducted during the inspection determined the substance was "extremely pure cocaine," officials said.

Border Officers Crack Open Cabbage Shipment Hiding Multimillion-dollar Secret At Texas Border

Authorities said the bust was one of the largest anti-drug operations carried out at the port in recent years.

The shipment could have generated an estimated 250 million euros, or nearly $290 million, if it had reached the street market.

Massive Mexico-california Border Busts Uncover Eye-popping Amount Of Drugs: 'Remain Vigilant'

The seizure followed an extensive analysis of commercial shipping routes linking South America with Vado Ligure, an important gateway for goods entering Europe — and an "attractive" target for international drug traffickers, according to officials.

"The operation forms part of an ongoing and targeted effort to combat the international trafficking of narcotics, including efforts to protect the most vulnerable segments of the population, who are often exposed to the harmful effects of this type of criminal activity," Guardia di Finanza said.

Jeanine Pirro Calls Out 'Dirtbags' After 14 Charged In Alleged Cocaine Ring Operating Feet From A Schoolyard

The seizure comes weeks after U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officers discovered more than 1,100 pounds of cocaine hidden in a commercial tractor-trailer carrying cabbage at the Pharr Port of Entry in Texas.

A canine inspection and nonintrusive imaging scan alerted officers to the concealed load, CBP said. A physical search of the trailer uncovered 477 packages containing 1,173 pounds of cocaine with an estimated street value of nearly $10.8 million.

Fox News Digital's Leo Briceno contributed to this report.

Original article source: $290M cocaine haul found 'skillfully concealed' in massive banana shipment

title: Farm's delight after six white owlets found nesting
description: The fluffy birds are estimated to be between 40 to 50 days old.
fullText:
A nest of six barn owlets has brought good news to a farm after a challenging period.

The fluffy birds - which are estimated to be between 40 to 50 days old - were discovered at Moor Farm, near Peterborough, and were ringed on Thursday as part of ongoing conservation work.

Judith Jacobs, the farm's owner, said the owlets were thriving and had lifted spirits after the farm was the target of six suspected arson attacks this summer.

Simon Dudhill, who monitors the birds for Peterborough City Council's barn owl recovery scheme, said finding six owlets sharing a nest was "exceptionally good" and was the largest brood he had encountered in 2026.

He added he had ringed more than 260 owlets in Cambridgeshire this year.

Each bird from Moor Farm was fitted with a metal ring carrying a unique identification number issued by the British Trust for Ornithology.

The ring's details allow conservationists to track individual birds and provides information about their movements.

Jacobs said: "It's very exciting as we had none last year. We try and encourage wildlife as much as we can.

"We have created a lot of hunting paths for the owls away from the main road, so they can hunt safely, because the biggest predators for barn owls are cars."

She said the six owlets, which include three females and three males, are each about three days apart in age.

"Simon measured the length of their fourth feather, which tells their age, and from the colour of their feathers, if they are spotty they are females and the non-spotty ones are males," she added.

Dudhill, who works for the Wildlife Conservation Partnership, said 2026 had been a much stronger breeding year for barn owls.

"I have ringed quite a few groups of five and four owlets this year. But this is the first brood of six I have found," he added.

"Over the last couple of years, we have seen a decline in the number of babies but not adult barn owls.

"Last year we found a lot of adults in boxes but they did not breed. This is all related to food. If there isn't enough food around, then they don't breed.

"This year has been completely different. I personally found barn owl numbers have increased dramatically. But there are still some areas in Cambridgeshire that have not found as many babies as I have," he said.

Despite monitoring barn owls for 15 years, Dudhill said the experience "never gets boring".

"When you open a box, you have one or two or three little faces looking at you. It's just as exciting now as the first time I opened a box," he added.

title: 1.6 million eggs recalled. See products, affected states
description: Midwest Poultry Services has recalled nearly 1.6 million white and brown eggs due to potential salmonella contamination.
fullText:
Nearly 1.6 million white-shell and brown-shell eggs have been recalled due to potential salmonella contamination.

Midwest Poultry Services, L.P. issued a recall on July 22 for nearly 1.6 million dozen white and brown cage-free eggs from Texas due to potential salmonella enteritidis contamination, according to a U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) recall report.

As of July 22, Midwest Poultry Services was not aware of any illnesses related to the eggs.

The company identified the issue through proactive environmental monitoring practices and root causes analysis, according to the recall report. The company was not distributing fresh eggs from its Texas farms as of July 22.

Here's what to know about the egg recall.

Food: Recall of popular potato chips classified under FDA's highest risk level

The recalled white and brown cage-free eggs have an identifying code printed on the left or right sides of the carton in date-coding ink. Only the codes P-1950 or 0840962 with a Julian date between 157 and 184 are recalled, according to the report.

Get a closer look of the recalled eggs below:

Can't see the table in your browser? Visit http://bit.ly/4wZqqVv.

The recalled eggs were sold in Kroger stores in Texas and Louisiana, Brookshire Grocery stores in Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, Louisiana, New Mexico and Mississippi, and at other smaller retail outlets, according to the recall report.

The eggs were also shipped to foodservice and retailers in Texas, Oklahoma and Louisiana, the report states.

Consumers are urged to avoid consumption of the eggs and return the products to their original place of purchase for a full refund, according to the recall report.

Consumers with questions should contact Midwest Poultry Services at 574-405-9531 between 8 a.m. and 4:30 p.m. ET Monday through Friday.

Symptoms of salmonella poisoning usually start six hours to six days after ingesting the bacteria. The most common symptoms are diarrhea, fever and stomach cramps, according to the CDC.

While most healthy people can recover in four to seven days without treatment, some vulnerable groups, like children under 5, adults 65 and older, and those with weakened immune systems may experience more severe illness that requires medical treatment or hospitalization.

If you display any of these more serious symptoms, the CDC advises contacting your health care professional:

Diarrhea and a fever higher than 102 degrees Fahrenheit

Diarrhea for more than three days that is not improving

So much vomiting that you cannot keep liquids down

Signs of dehydration, such as not urinating much, dry mouth and throat, and feeling dizzy when standing up

Contributing: Mary Walrath-Holdridge, USA TODAY

Greta Cross is a national trending reporter at USA TODAY. Story idea? Email her at gcross@usatoday.com.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY Unlimited Use: 1.6 million eggs recalled. See products, affected states

title: 'I woke up in the night to my field ablaze'
description: Steven Conisbee, from Fetcham, says the speed at which flames engulfed his land was "alarming".
fullText:
A farmer has described feeling "absolutely gutted" following a blaze on land.

Steven Conisbee, who runs Barracks Farm in Fetcham, said he awoke on Friday at 22:00 to some loud bangs and then immediately after his son called to say the farm was on fire.

"I leapt out of bed, threw some clothes on, jumped in my pickup truck and headed down," he told the BBC.

"I thought the barn was on fire, but the sky was lit up orange."

Conisbee, a ninth-generation farmer, continued that he discovered his "best wheat field" was ablaze.

"The wind was blowing," he told the BBC.

"The noise which really took me by surprise and the speed with which the flames were engulfing [the field]."

"I really thought it was going to be a big problem".

Conisbee estimated that he had lost around £7,500 worth of wheat from the fire, which was eventually put out by firefighters and people on the farm.

"Farming's hard enough as it is - at the moment there is nothing in it.

"To grow an... actually promising crop [on a drought year] and then to watch it disappear before your eyes - I felt absolutely gutted.

He added he was "very, very" concerned about future fires happening again on his Surrey farm as the area is a "total tinderbox".

However, Conisbee continued: "As a farmer I'm an eternal optimist."

"We'll plough [the field], sow this autumn and go again."

It comes as fire services across the South East have warned people about the risk of wildfires.

Following a blaze at Ashdown Forest - which inspired the Winnie-the-Pooh series - East Sussex Fire and Rescue Service urged the public not to light fires and BBQs or drop cigarette butts.

There have been a number of wildfires on the South Downs this summer, including near Eastbourne and at Devil's Dyke in Sussex, alongside Chobham Common, Whitmoor Common and Brentmoor Heath in Surrey.

On Thursday, Surrey Fire and Rescue Service said it had contained another wildfire at Brentmoor Heath near Woking.
