title: Chris Sale slated to start series finale versus San Diego
description: The Braves seek win #60 to secure the series win.
fullText:
Braves brunch baseball is back on the menu, folks.

The things to like about last night’s game? Runs on the board for the good guys (albeit late), ultimately not snatching defeat from the jaws of victory, and guaranteeing a series split.

No need to relive the bad or discuss the ninth inning other than the totally normal Iggy save.

The Braves have a golden opportunity to win a series against the Padres at home for the first time since 2018 (!) with their ace on the mound.

At this point in the season with our rollercoaster rotation, it almost brings tears to the eyes to see Chris Sale set to start. Sale (10-6, 2.06 ERA) twirled a gem in his last time out with seven shutout innings against the Texas Rangers. He threw 89 pitches, only surrendered two hits, and struck out six in a rare occasion for Braves starters this season: pitching 6+ innings. He also recorded career strikeout #2,700.

Other than that, July’s been a mixed bag for Chris with the frustrating rain-shortened outing in St. Louis and the five innings of three-run ball versus the Mets in the July 4th laugher.

The Padres did not face Sale when the Braves were in San Diego, but Manny Machado leads the team in ABs against him (11-for-35, .355 AVG, and .993 OPS). Xander Bogaerts is next-highest, 7-for-22. They have a homer apiece. Fernando Tatis Jr. and Gavin Sheets also have a homer off Sale in a smaller sample size. We’re hoping for more of what Jake Cronenworth has going on: he’s 0-for-10 with 6 K’s.

For the finale, the Padres will call upon starter Griffin Canning (1-8, 6.67 ERA). The former Angel/Met struggled mightily against the Braves when he faced them on June 23. Wandy Peralta served as an effective opener, but Canning only recorded two outs in the second inning. He gave up three earned runs before departing, and a fourth one scored after Kyle Hart walked in an inherited runner. Canning’s career versus the Braves: 7.2 IP, one loss, and a 12.91 ERA.

Since that game in San Diego, Canning hasn’t fared much better. The longest he’s lasted in his last four starts is 4.2 innings. His best outing in that bunch is when he held the Dodgers to one run in four innings of work.

With Sale pitching and the reemergence of the Braves offense in July, this one has the makings of a decisive win #60 to take the series and end the homestand on a high note. We’ll see what the lunchtime baseball has in store.

Game Date/Time: Thursday, July 23, 12:15 p.m. ET

Location: Truist Park, Atlanta, GA

Radio: 680 AM / 93.7 FM The Fan, Los Bravos

title: Will Man United sign Aurelien Tchouameni? Why Real Madrid midfielder now looks like attainable transfer target
description: Aurelien Tchouameni had looked likely to sign a new Real Madrid contract. Suddenly, a move to the Premier League is back on the table.
fullText:
Will Man United sign Aurelien Tchouameni? Why Real Madrid midfielder now looks like attainable transfer target originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Another year, another transfer window where Manchester United attempt to sign midfielders.

Except this time, they're actually getting them.

Michael Carrick made it clear that strengthening the heart of the team was a priority, and they have already brought in Andrey Santos and Youri Tielemans. Their next target would appear to be Aurelien Tchouameni of Real Madrid.

Rumours of interest in Tchouameni surfaced before the 2026 World Cup, but nothing really came of it. However, recent reports indicate United's interest never really went away and, in fact, they may now be in a stronger position to strike what would likely be one of the biggest deals of the window — perhaps one that even rivals the fee Chelsea paid for Morgan Rogers.

Could it actually happen? Let's look at the latest.

MORE:Man United's preseason schedule as Michael Carrick aims for strong start

The Sun's Samuel Luckhurst reported on July 23 that Madrid may now consider selling Tchouameni to Man United, despite previous indications that they would resist all but the most outlandish bids.

United are known to want another midfielder, ideally one that is a specialist defensive player, despite signing Andrey Santos from Chelsea and Tielemans from Aston Villa. The Red Devils' priority is a No. 6 in the approximate age range of 22-26, and ideally one with plenty of top-level experience already. Tchouameni, a La Liga, Champions League and Club World Cup winner who has played at two World Cups for France, fits the bill.

There is a long way to go before this can be considered very likely to happen, but the chances of a deal being struck would seem to be higher than they were a month ago.

It was previously reported that Madrid were eager to keep Tchouameni, and talk even emerged that he had agreed to a contract extension. Now, however, The Sun says the Spanish club would be prepared to listen to offers.

Madrid have already brought in Bernardo Silva on a free transfer and have Federico Valverde and Eduardo Camavinga on the books. It is also believed they are making every effort to sign Manchester City midfielder Rodri, who has a year left on his contract but appears keen to return to Spain.

🚨 Excl. - Agreement in principle for a contract until 2030 between #Rodri and #RealMadrid, which are ready to open talks to try to sign him from #ManchesterCity. #MCFC would like to keep him and offered the contract extension, but the midfielder has not accepted. #transfers

— Nicolò Schira (@NicoSchira) July 23, 2026

Arda Guler and Jude Bellingham are also capable of playing as part of a midfield pivot if required, so selling Tchouameni to balance the books and reduce squad bloating would make sense, even if new coach Jose Mourinho would be happy if he stayed. Madrid could also make further additions in this window if they can command a big enough transfer fee for the France international.

Few concrete figures have been suggested when it comes to United and Tchouameni, partly because no formal transfer bid has yet been submitted.

There was a report from Miguel Serrano just prior to the World Cup that Madrid would only listen to offers of around £153.4m ($205.7m), but it is unlikely the Red Devils would sanction such an exorbitant spend on a 26-year-old with two years left on his contract.

Madrid paid £72.5m ($89.7m) for Tchouameni to sign him from Monaco in 2022, so they would want at least to recoup that sum in full. An offer in the region of £90m ($120m) guaranteed might get negotiations to advance.

Tchouameni was allegedly involved in a "very serious" altercation with teammate Federico Valverde at Madrid's training ground in early May 2026.

Reports at the time indicated that matters escalated to the point of a fight between the two, during which Valverde slipped and banged his head on a table. The Uruguayan was later treated in hospital.

The club fined both players €500,000, but Valverde issued a statement insisting the incident had been blown out of proportion. Tchouameni, while not denying that some sort of altercation occurred, dismissed the "huge reaction" in the media, adding: "A lot of nonsense was said in the press."

MORE:Full details on training-ground dustup between Valverde and Tchouameni

United have made three signings to date in this transfer window: goalkeeper Karl Darlow, and midfielders Tielemans and Santos.

Darlow was a free transfer after his contract at Leeds United expired. He was brought in to provide additional goalkeeping depth, with Andre Onana having returned to Trabzonspor on loan and Altay Bayindir expected to be sold.

Tielemans and Santos cost a combined £83m ($110.8m) in initial fees. United have not yet sold any players to offset the expense, but they did earn £10.57m ($14.1m) as part of a clause in Mason Greenwood's Marseille contract. Greenwood was sold by the French club to Fenerbahce this window.

Mason Greenwood*
*Income via sell-on clause

title: Yankees Make Spencer Jones Roster Move Before Trade Deadline
description: The New York Yankees have quietly moved their top-ranked prospect outfielder following trade reports.
fullText:
The New York Yankees entered the final stretch before the MLB trade deadline with one of baseball's deepest farm systems and a front office facing difficult decisions. As the club continues weighing whether to move top prospects in pursuit of another postseason run, one of its most recognizable young players has remained at the center of speculation.

Hulking outfielder Spencer Jones has long been viewed as a cornerstone prospect thanks to his rare combination of size, athleticism and power. But after multiple trips between Triple-A and the majors this season, his future in the organization has become a popular topic as rival executives search for impact young talent and the Yankees look to upgrade other areas of the roster before the trade deadline.

FORBES | By Peter Chawaga
Cubs’ Craig Counsell Sends Front Office Message Shortly Before Trade Deadline

Industry chatter intensified this week when Jones' name surfaced in reports discussing the Yankees' deadline strategy.

"Spencer Jones and Jasson Dominguez are two of the key pieces the Yankees are planning to use as trade assets ahead of the Trade Deadline, according to industry sources,” MLB insider Francys Romero reported on X.

Whether New York ultimately parts with either of those promising young outfielders remains to be seen, but the report immediately fueled discussion surrounding Jones' place in the organization's long-term plans.

And that speculation gained another layer on Thursday, when the Yankees made another roster move involving the 24-year-old outfielder, moving him back down to their Triple-A affiliate just a few hours after calling him up to the big-league roster, according to the official transaction log.

FORBES | By Peter Chawaga
Trump Picks Replacement For Mets’ Steve Cohen Amid Historic Losing

Jones' latest demotion follows a very brief and unheralded opportunity to establish himself at the major league level, suggesting he might be falling out of favor in the Bronx.

“Spencer Jones made his big-league debut to great fanfare on May 8 in Milwaukee, having been called up from the minors when Jasson Dominguez landed on the injured list,” Erik Boland reported for Newsday. “Jones, considered a can't-miss Yankees prospect, much in the way Dominguez was at one point, was recalled to the majors for Wednesday's doubleheader against the (Pittsburgh) Pirates to significantly less fanfare. And this time around, his stay in the big leagues, his third stint this season, was assured to be a brief one.”

Indeed, the stint lasted less than 24 hours.

Through 31 major league games this season, Jones is batting just .224 with two home runs and a .669 OPS. His strikeout rate has remained a significant obstacle, with 35 strikeouts in only 76 at-bats.

For now, the Yankees appear focused on giving Jones additional at-bats in Triple-A while simultaneously evaluating their deadline options. Whether he returns to the Bronx later this season or becomes part of a blockbuster deal could be one of the organization's biggest storylines before the trade deadline passes.

This article was originally published on Forbes.com

title: Leeds United chasing deal for Manchester City goalkeeper
description: Leeds United are looking for a new shot stopper, which has led them towards Manchester City's James Trafford.
fullText:
Leeds United chasing deal for Manchester City goalkeeper originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Leeds United have had one priority on their to-do list during the opening months of the Summer transfer window: get a new goalkeeper. This priority comes after the departure of Illan Meslier, who departed Elland Road for a free transfer to the Emirates in North London with Arsenal, leaving The Peacocks with a gaping hole in between the sticks.

One of the initial names linked with Leeds United was that of Zion Suzuki, who at the time was on the global stage with the Japanese National Team at the 2026 FIFA World Cup, recording a lone clean sheet during The Samurai Blue's Round of 32 run in the tournament. But after Leed's bid for the Japanese keeper fell short of what Parma requires, they've been forced to look elsewhere.

According to a recent report, their search for a keeper has gone from international to domestic, this time looking towards Manchester City.

As reported by The Athletic's David Ornstein, the Peacocks have identified James Trafford as their new target, having recently reached out to the Sky Blues over a move for their back-up keeper. Trafford made his way back to Manchester City, his boyhood club, last season as the Mancunian club looked for a replacement for the recently departed Ederson.

The Englishman would start in between the sticks for the Cityzens' opening slate of games, before the team made a move for Gianluigi Donnarumma before the transfer window shut, effectively reducing Trafford's role on the team to Cup-starter or back-up keeper. When all was said and done, Trafford started 17 games, recording eight clean sheets and 39 saves.

It's not known what the price tag is hanging over Trafford's head by Manchester City, but the two clubs are in discussions. Leeds United aren't the first team tied to Trafford in recent weeks, as Newcastle United were linked with a move, but have opted to move elsewhere.

If Trafford were to depart the Etihad, it would leave the Cityzens with only Marcus Bettinelli to back up their Italian shot-stopper.

title: Esteban Ocon: "Nothing to announce" on F1 future amid tough 2026
description: Ocon's seat at Haas is under threat for 2027 after a difficult 18 months with the F1 squad
fullText:
Esteban Ocon confirmed he has “nothing to announce yet” regarding his Formula 1 future after the most difficult start to a season of his career.

The Haas driver has scored just three points across the opening 10 rounds of the 2026 campaign, claiming to struggle with the inconsistencies of the VF-26.

But it has just been a continuation of his poor form from 2025, a season in which he finished 15th in the standings and three points behind rookie team-mate Oliver Bearman - who is already 15 clear this year.

So, with Ocon’s contract expiring at the end of 2026, his seat is under threat for 2027 and Haas is understood to be considering Leonardo Fornaroli or Rafael Camara as Bearman's next team-mate.

Why nationality should be 'no factor' as Haas considers Fornaroli and Camara for F1 2027

It was therefore put to Ocon ahead of this weekend’s Hungarian Grand Prix, which is the final race before the annual summer shutdown where driver signings for the following season are often confirmed.

“Of course nothing to announce yet,” said the 29-year-old. “The summer break will be quite busy. We will see. At the moment we're focused on this race and we'll see what happens.

“There's always many options. At the moment we will discuss with the team first. I've been here for two years. I've been convinced by Ayao [Komatsu] to come here. The first discussion will happen with this team for sure.”

The 2021 Hungarian GP victor has simply underwhelmed since joining Haas from Alpine for 2025, particularly as he became the American outfit’s first ever race-winning driver.

Ocon was expected to lead the squad forward in this new era, but the veteran has struggled to put a run of results together and is being comfortably beaten by his 21-year-old team-mate.

The Frenchman even previously claimed that the inconsistencies of the VF-26 had hindered him at all but two rounds in 2026 - his only points finishes came in Japan and Monaco - marking Ocon’s worst start since his F1 debut in 2016.

So when asked in Budapest to summarise his season so far, he replied: “Not frustration maybe, but too many difficulties. Too many issues. Too many things that prevented us from performing.

“Probably the most difficult first half of the season that I've ever had. In terms of how consistent the car was, from one race to another, and how many problems I had this year.

“I hope it will get better. We are working hard for it to get better, all together, and it's not only tough for me, it's tough for the whole team.

“But we know what we have to do and we are sticking together to fix the problems. That's the important thing.”

Five things to look out for at the F1 Hungarian GP

title: George Russell Cleared of Blame as Mercedes Issues Fix for Hungary
description: Following a catastrophic early exit at the Belgian Grand Prix, George Russell was vocal over the team radio, labeling his car’s sudden loss of straight-line speed “unacceptable.” It turns out, his anger was entirely justified, and the culprit wasn’t mechanical—it…
fullText:
Following a catastrophic early exit at the Belgian Grand Prix, George Russell was vocal over the team radio, labeling his car’s sudden loss of straight-line speed “unacceptable.” It turns out, his anger was entirely justified, and the culprit wasn’t mechanical—it was digital.

As highlighted in a recent social media update from @formularacers, Mercedes engineers have tracked the severe power deployment issues at Spa down to a glitch buried “deep in the code” of Russell’s W17.

With a software patch reportedly implemented ahead of this weekend’s Hungarian Grand Prix, the paddock is getting a clearer picture of exactly how a few lines of bad code destroyed a race weekend.

Formula 1’s 2026 regulations rely heavily on complex electrical deployment maps, in which software algorithms dictate exactly when and where a driver receives a battery boost.

According to a detailed breakdown by The Race, the bug in Russell’s software essentially front-loaded his energy deployment. The algorithm was heavily biased toward burning extra energy early in the lap, leaving the battery completely flat by the time he reached the massive acceleration zones on the Kemmel Straight and the run down to the final chicane. This is why Russell was bleeding critical tenths of a second to his teammate, Kimi Antonelli, in qualifying, despite identical machinery.

This digital glitch had severe physical consequences on race day. During the opening lap, a separate but related software error prevented Russell’s car from harvesting adequate energy braking into La Source (Turn 1).

When Russell charged up Eau Rouge and crested Raidillon, he expected a massive surge of electrical power to defend his position. Instead, the battery was empty. The sudden and unexpected drop in momentum threw him right into the clutches of the chasing pack. That lack of speed directly contributed to his race-ending tangle with Lewis Hamilton at Les Combes, sending Russell spinning into the gravel trap.

Russell’s DNF highlights a growing frustration in the new regulatory era: the overwhelming dominance of the algorithm. As the Associated Press points out, a simple coding error can now cripple a car just as easily as a blown turbo or a shattered gearbox, creating mysteries that even the drivers cannot diagnose from the cockpit.

With Antonelli capitalizing on the chaos to secure his sixth win of the season and extend his massive championship lead, Russell simply cannot afford another digital misstep. Fortunately, with the countermeasures in place, the Hungaroring should offer a much-needed reset for the Brit.

title: Arsenal sign Christos Tzolis as £34m Leandro Trossard replacement
description: Arsenal’s third permanent summer transfer comes 48 hours after the north Londoners missed Morgan Rogers
fullText:
Arsenal have finalised the £34m acquisition of Greece international Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge.

The 24-year-old has signed a five-year contract with the Premier League champions, replacing Leandro Trossard following his Besiktas switch.

Tzolis’ move to the Emirates Stadium – Arsenal’s third permanent summer transfer – comes 48 hours after the north Londoners missed Morgan Rogers, who signed a £117m British-record fee with Chelsea, describing his new employers as the "biggest club in London".

Tzolis scored 43 times in 108 appearances for Club Brugge, and notably featured in the Belgians’ 3-0 Champions League defeat to Mikel Arteta’s side last season.

Arsenal’s sporting director Andrea Berta said: “We’re delighted to have completed the signing of Christos Tzolis.

“Christos is an extremely versatile attacking player who naturally operates on the left but is comfortable across the front line. He’s an excellent finisher with both feet, thrives in tight spaces, and possesses incredible technical ability.

“Christos has produced outstanding numbers in terms of goals and assists over the last three seasons, and he’s a player who will raise the technical level of our squad, while bringing positive energy, enthusiasm, and a strong mentality to our team.

“We’re confident Christos will make a significant contribution to our club, and we hope our supporters will enjoy watching him play in an Arsenal shirt. We look forward to sharing this exciting new chapter together.”

Tzolis played 14 Premier League games for Norwich during the 2021-22 campaign but did not find the back of the net. Loan periods with FC Twente and Fortuna Dusseldorf followed before he made the move to Club Brugge in 2024.

Tzolis follows Piero Hincapie – who turned his temporary deal with Arsenal into a full-time switch – and goalkeeper Illan Meslier, a free signing from Leeds, as the Gunners’ third signing.

The announcement arrives as a boost of sorts for Arteta after defender William Saliba was this week ruled out for an “extensive period” with a back problem which curtailed his World Cup. The France international is set to miss the start of Arsenal’s Premier League defence.

title: COMMENTARY | Just Junt: 3 things to watch for when Idaho begins fall football camp next week
description: Jul. 23—It's finally time. Idaho will begin fall football camp in less than a week. The first practice is scheduled for 9:30 a.m.
fullText:
Jul. 23—It's finally time. Idaho will begin fall football camp in less than a week.

The first practice is scheduled for 9:30 a.m. Wednesday, when the Vandal football team will be on the field practicing as a unit for the first time ahead of its 2026 campaign.

Here are three of the biggest things I will be watching for at the beginning of the '26 fall camp:

Offensive coordinator Ian Shoemaker will be junior quarterback Joshua Wood's third play-caller in as many years.

One thing Shoemaker mentioned during spring camp is that he wants to test Wood and the rest of the quarterbacks to their limits.

That means a very fast-paced offensive scheme with RPOs (run-pass options) that will force the quarterback to make extremely quick reads and try to make the correct decisions.

If Wood is struggling at the beginning of camp, that's no issue at all. But if he is struggling two or three weeks into the new system, then there's a problem.

Now, last year Wood stepped into previous offensive coordinator Matt Linehan's system and looked like the best player on the field almost instantly.

Maybe, but it's more realistic to think it may take a little bit more adjusting to a more complicated system.

Wood is the engine of the offense and is crucial to the Vandals' success. If he can't get it going eventually in fall camp, the U of I football team is facing some trouble.

However, on the flip side, if he looks extremely comfortable, making all the right decisions and dominating camp, Idaho is in for a really fun season.

New additions in the running back room

Idaho coach Thomas Ford Jr. is a running backs guy through and through.

He played running back at the premier Division III football team in the Pacific Northwest at Linfield University and coached the same position group there a few years after graduating. Ford also coached running backs at Idaho from 2022-23, along with being the special teams coordinator during that tenure.

Then he went on to coach running backs at Oregon State in 2024, right before he joined the Vandals as the head coach.

The new additions to the running back room for this upcoming season seem like guys that Ford may have handpicked.

The two junior transfer running backs, Ferrari Miller and Damonte Bias, are guys that I expect the Vandals will lean on.

Miller is 5-foot-10 and listed at 220 pounds from Diablo Valley College in Pleasant Hill, Calif., while Bias is listed at 5-11, 180 pounds from El Camino College in Alondra Park, Calif.

Both were productive at their last stops.

In 2025, Miller played 12 games and rushed the ball 179 times for a total of 1,123 yards (93.6 yards per game) and scored eight touchdowns. Miller also caught the ball 39 times for 384 yards (32 yards per game) and added another five touchdowns through the air.

Last season, Bias played in 11 games and rushed the ball 118 times for a total of 835 yards (75.9 yards per game) and scored six touchdowns.

As Shoemaker put it in the spring, Miller is the thunder and Bias is the lightning.

But one of the more exciting additions to this running back room is Kenny Golston, the freshman from Valliant High School in Valliant, Okla.

Golston was a three-star commit and had offers from Indiana, Missouri, Oregon and North Texas, yet landed on the Vandals' roster.

It wouldn't be too surprising for him not to see much action right off the bat, but with Golston being such a highly-touted recruit, I would not be shocked if he is in the action immediately.

He could be a difference-maker if given the chance.

This Idaho defense looks a whole lot different than it did last year.

The defense has a new coordinator in Lee Stalker and lost many of their main leaders in Dylan Layne (Oregon State) and Matyus McLain (Washington State) to the transfer portal, and Isiah King and Zach Krotzer to graduation.

Now the defense will be reliant on new leaders.

Here are a few guys that could slide into that role: fifth-year linebacker Cruz Hepburn, fifth-year defensive lineman Mitchel Jaskowiak and junior safety Zach Wusstig.

Those are guys who made a significant impact last year and can seemingly lead by example through tough practices and hard play, and some could be very vocal.

Hepburn, a Lewiston High product, will be one of the more important pieces on this defense as the main linebacker and the No. 1 option as the expected main play-caller on that side of the ball.

In 2025, Hepburn was third on the team in tackles with 71 and in tackles for loss with six.

Jaskowiak could fill the Krotzer role as a defensive tackle that helps move the D-line in the right direction. He played all 12 games last year and accumulated 22 tackles. With some experience and a need for someone to step up in that position, Jaskowiak would be the player I expect.

The Vandals will need players from all three phases of the defense to lead, and I think that Wusstig would make a lot of sense here.

Wusstig played 11 games last season and totaled 49 tackles, 2 1/2 for loss, and defended two passes. With former play-caller and safety Hayden John now with UC Davis, a safety will probably call the plays from the secondary. It would make sense for Wusstig to grow into that spot.

But it also could be senior safety Khaled Rawls, who recently transitioned from cornerback during the spring.

There are also some transfers I could see filling leadership roles, similar to what defensive lineman Donovan Parham did last season.

A few that make sense: senior defensive ends Jamarion Augustus (Northern Iowa) and Javen Augustus (Coastal Carolina) and junior linebacker Darrell Gipson Jr. (Cincinnati).

Junt can be reached at 208-848-2258, tjunt@lmtribune.com or on X @TrevorJunt.

title: 3 Ups & 4 Downs From WWE Unreal Season 3 (Review)
description: Looking at the good and bad of the latest season of WWE Unreal.
fullText:
WWE Unreal is back for a third series, available now as part of the company's deal with Netflix. The controversial reality TV show has had a hit-and-miss response from fans, but this new season concentrates on some enormous stories and angles.

Covered as part of Season 3 of Unreal, John Cena's retirement match and his farewell tour take center stage in the opening few episodes. Bron Breakker, Liv Morgan, Oba Femi, Trick Williams, and Stephanie Vaquer are given special coverage, while Cody Rhodes and Randy Orton's controversial WrestleMania 42 main event and AJ Lee's return to wrestling are given the WWE Unreal treatment.

As has been the case in the first two seasons, WWE Unreal has moments of genuine revelation interspersed with the company's need to protect its decision-making process. With the stakes as high as they've ever been, how well does WWE's in-house reality show cover one of the most controversial periods in the company's recent history?

When the first season of Unreal aired, it was can't-miss TV. For the first time, WWE was set to acknowledge the complexities of its backstage workings, secrets that had been kept for decades, and bring us access to the people behind the characters they play on TV. Across the show's first two seasons, fan expectation has been tempered. Unreal is part of the company's mission to be seen as entertainment that overcomes any adversity to deliver flawlessly to its fans, who are wowed by WWE's every move.

Fans have begrudgingly accepted the show is an exercise in PR, but Season 3 has a harder job of portraying WWE's ability to please its audience than in its first two seasons.

This year's WrestleMania was controversial for all the wrong reasons, as constant adverts made for a disjointed viewing experience, shortened matches were panned, and poorly executed angles came to largely unsatisfying conclusions. The John Cena retirement tour was also considered a disappointment, with his final match against Gunther at Saturday Night's Main Event causing weeks of hot takes about the man who never gives up tapping out. Season 3 of Unreal concentrates on trying to discredit these negatives with pretty unsatisfactory results.

Everything else is business as usual. Fly-on-the-wall footage is fun, and fans feel like they get a better idea of the personalities they follow. AJ Lee, Liv Morgan, and Trick Williams benefit best from this, while Stephanie Vaquer and Bron Breakker's coverage is a little disappointing, with the discussion around Breakker not being the right fit to win the 2026 Royal Rumble being one of the only moments that feel like you're getting any genuine look behind the curtain. Paul Levesque is worried fans might feel like they're having Breakker rammed down their throats, instead plumping for a man who's never been pushed down their throats: Roman Reigns. Sure.

From what news cycles have told us, TKO's meddling in night one of WrestleMania 42's main event is why the story around Cody Rhodes vs. Randy Orton for the WWE Championship went down like a fart at a wedding. By making Pat McAfee the focal point of their feud, adding in Jelly Roll for some celebrity spectacle, and ignoring a 15-year history between Rhodes and Orton, fans were bewildered by how a feud that felt like it couldn't fail went so spectacularly wrong.

Unreal does a better job of selling the history of Randy and Cody in the first five mins of episode five than the six weeks that led to their match in Las Vegas. As Cody walks around the WWE Warehouse, looking at famous artifacts of years gone by, he is shown his trunks from his first 'Mania, a triple threat with The Viper and Ted DiBiase Jnr. He speaks expertly of their long-running history together, as clips amplify the story he's telling.

Cody Rhodes is one of the best talkers in wrestling, and he understands every facet of the business, so when he tells us WrestleMania 42 is his favourite WrestleMania match since he came back, he will surely know that not a soul will believe him. Fans everywhere know that TKO drove Rhodes and Orton’s main event into a wall, but there’s no way WWE's "QB1" is going to blast its parent company on the world's biggest streaming platform. One of WWE's biggest stars lying through his teeth does not make for great reality television.

It's telling that Pat McAfee and Jelly Roll aren't interviewed for Unreal, and neither is Randy Orton. McAfee's role in the feud is almost entirely ignored, in exchange for showing close-ups of fans smiling and high-fiving as the match unfolded. It is a stark contrast to how people feel about the feud and match, and is one of the reasons Unreal is quickly becoming a point of ridicule for wrestling fans everywhere. It is, at best, a dishonest appraisal of how fans felt about a match they'd waited years for, and, at worst, a rank piece of propaganda.

For fans coming into Season 3 of WWE Unreal, the biggest question revolved around "how is WWE going to spin the John Cena farewell tour as not a disaster?"

The most considerable way WWE combats that perception is to lean into John Cena, the man. It is no secret to WWE fans that Cena has always been a one-of-one when it comes to being the model professional who personifies everything you could ever want as the face of a billion-dollar, publicly traded enterprise. Unreal's job is to let the outside world know who Cena is, which is done by his faultless soundbites and general selflessness.

To nobody's surprise, Cena is all class, all the time. John himself is keen to sell himself as just a man who did the most with his opportunity. From working with kids to sharing his knowledge with members of the roster, to giving a pep talk to Trick Williams and Oba Femi at their first WrestleMania, this is great PR for letting the world know what a good example John Cena sets for others.

From his long-running affinity for CM Punk, how gracious he is with Liv Morgan on her big return, to giving fans what they wanted between him and AJ Styles, the relationship he’s shared with WWE cameraman Stew, and how that’s a mechanism to share his joy with the people watching at home, there is a notion that John Cena’s farewell tour was all about giving back. Unreal furthers that narrative every step of the way.

There's an enjoyable look at the creativity of Cena too, as he wanted to really ruin wrestling for fans during his heel turn. By making his appearances and matches as unsatisfying as possible, John was succeeding in being a "true heel". It's an interesting take, and one that Levesque has to force him to reconsider, as it was working so well that fans were getting mad at WWE as a result.

The decision around TKO board member The Rock vanishing from our screens after being the catalyst for Cena's heel turn and the complete disappearance of Travis Scott after WrestleMania 41 is completely ignored. As fans, we can't magically forget that this happened, and if Unreal is to have more value as a "Reality TV show", it has to do a better job of addressing the big questions that exist for fans around the incidents that they cover.

WWE feels determined to let fans know just how hard they have been rocked by injuries over the course of the last year. Paul Levesque tries spinning that injuries for some are opportunities for others, but the soundbites gathered around Seth Rollins's course-altering injury at Crown Jewel suggest that WrestleMania 42's plans were enormously impacted by his absence in the months leading up to the event.

It could be reading between the lines a little, but Unreal seems to show WWE holding its hands up and admitting that The Vision has not worked due to everybody getting injured. Bron Breakker is one of the series' main focus points, but his hernia injury curtails his impact on the product that was intended to be captured. Bronson Reed and Austin Theory are caught in candid conversation after Bron tore his bicep, with both suggesting that The Vision is cursed.

When fans follow the product every week and judge a show like WrestleMania on what happens on the night itself, it can be easy to forget that WWE has had a nightmare year with injuries to some of its marquee talents.

For a show justifiedly belittled for the lack of reality in its Reality TV, the love story around CM Punk and AJ Lee, and the heartbreaking passing of their dog Larry, is incredibly moving viewing.

The first few episodes concentrate on AJ's return to wrestling, and the love that exists between herself and Punk. They both glow when talking about one another, their affection doesn't ever feel performative, and Lee reveals that they agreed to do an angle on camera together because after “12 years of privacy, it felt like the right time to celebrate my awesome husband and what we’ve done in wrestling.” Her return to the ring and her enjoyment in the current WWE system is satisfying, considering the unequal landscape she was formerly part of.

In episode four, these feel-good moments come to a gut-wrenching halt with the death of their dog, Larry. Larry was well known to the wrestling community, and his passing is something that will resonate with fans of all ages, races, genders, and sexualities. The mourning of Phil Brooks and April Mendez is uncomfortable to watch, just because the grief in their loss weighs a ton. It’s bold and brave for them to share the loss of their family member, and it redresses the balance of a world that doesn't do enough to recognize that the passing of our four-legged family members can hurt every bit as much as those who walk on two.

Punk’s valiant attempts to stop his grief becoming “just part of a reality show” are crushing to behold, as he doesn't want to talk to Netflix's crew about the pain he's going through. When talking about getting Larry’s paw print tattooed on his chest, he describes the day Larry left Earth as “the worst day of [his] life”. There is some relief as WWE, Punk, AJ, and fans around the world donated in their thousands to PAWS dog shelter in Chicago in Larry's honor, but this segment of the show is one of the few Unreal moments that fans feel part of a real-life look into the performers' lives.

Liv Morgan is maybe the most enjoyable story told by Unreal so far. Including enough storytelling to endear her to any Netflix viewer watching through curiosity, Morgan’s rags-to-riches story of busting her ass to go from Hooters waitress to one of the best wrestlers today is magnetic. Factor in her infectious laugh, a personality that is radically different from her on-screen persona, and her wholesome home life with cows, chickens, and other assorted animals, and fans get a snapshot of the real Liv Morgan that’s more valuable than the WWE propaganda that fills most of the show’s runtime.

The goodwill Liv builds makes it all the more affecting when she tearily tells the story of her big 2025 injury, and how she had to miss a stadium show in her hometown of New Jersey for last year’s SummerSlam. This is contrasted by her victory in the 2026 women's Royal Rumble, the fun she has performing her new entrance music at WrestleMania 42, and her coronation as Women's World Champion in Las Vegas.

Elsewhere, Trick Williams and Lash Legend's romance is heart-warming, as is the pride of Trick's mum (The Anointed Mum, if you will) in the aftermath of his US Championship victory at WrestleMania 42. Oba Femi's inevitable ascension is shown in a writer's room that is brimming with praise about his future, and Bron Breakker's dedication to fulfilling his destiny is a welcome aside.

Unreal does a good job of capturing the enormity of John Cena's final match against Gunther at 2025's final Saturday Night's Main Event in Washington. The emotion of the evening is personified by Charles Robinson bawling his eyes out before the match, CM Punk and Bayley bouncing around and hugging like fans while they enjoy Cena's finale, Michael Cole choking back tears on commhttps://whatculture.com/wwe/tom-brady-wwe-in-ongoing-conversations-over-future-appearance-wwe-newscelentary, and Al Green’s beautiful ‘For The Good Times’ soundtracking his final moments as he tapped out.

WWE could have just sold Cena’s final night as an in-ring talent as a beloved triumph, but they address people’s bemusement and anger in the booking of his final moment. Cena takes ownership of the story, saying that he knew his final moment tapping out to Gunther would not be universally liked. He explains why he decided to tap out, citing that some people are lucky enough to die surrounded by loved ones with a smile on their face, and that’s how he wanted to end his in-ring career.

The idea is that Cena's retirement in this particular way was done to make Gunther a bigger heel as a result. That worked for a few weeks, as Gunther went on to retire AJ Styles at the Royal Rumble, but fans can be forgiven for questioning this long-term, given his booking in 2026. He is about to fight SmackDown's GM at SummerSlam after being thrown haphazardly into a match with Seth Rollins at WrestleMania 42 that held little weight with fans whatsoever.

Unreal also addresses fans' displeasure with the booking of Cena’s retirement directly at Paul Levesque. Levesque says he considers Cena’s farewell run as “a success on all levels”. This is where Unreal falls apart. Through the opening two episodes, Cena’s failed heel run is barely mentioned, and the role Travis Scott and The Rock played in that run falling apart is not mentioned at all. This reality show too often ignores the biggest questions and criticisms fans have of WWE, in the name of making the company look like it always finds a way to succeed (despite all the evidence to the contrary).

All Future PLC brands are supported by its audience. When you purchase through the links on this article, Future PLC may earn an affiliate commission.

title: Injured UFC champion takes delivery driver job: ‘Whatever it takes’
description: The ex-strawweight champion, a 30-fight UFC veteran, has been recovering from a shoulder injury since last competing in August
fullText:
UFC star Jessica Andrade has revealed she has taken a job as a delivery driver, in a bid to pay the bills while she is injured.

The former strawweight champion, a 30-fight UFC veteran, last fought in August, suffering a shoulder injury in her loss to Loopy Godinez.

The result marked a third consecutive loss for the Brazilian, 34, who held the strawweight belt from May 2019 until August of the same year.

And while Andrade is unable to earn money through fighting, until she has reached full fitness and can compete in the UFC again, she has been working as a delivery driver and helping her friend with a landscaping project.

“Thank God, I managed to save a little money, so it’s going well,” Andrade said on the Direto de Vegas podcast. “But when needed, I work delivering. I go and deliver meals, things like that.

“So actually, it’s more or less that. You get paid through the app and you do the deliveries, and that’s how it goes. I manage.

“I’m not lazy when it comes to work. I can handle anything. Speaking of which, I recently started a project over at Rebecca’s house to put down some new grass.

“I said: ‘I’m going to do a test. If I’m good at this, it becomes one more thing that we can add if we ever find ourselves running a little short on cash, we need some extra money.’

“I feel like God is opening the way [...] and it helps to strengthen [my shoulder], right?”

Andrade’s story follows that of another Brazilian champion, Alexandre Pantoja, who worked as a delivery driver during Covid before winning the men’s flyweight title in 2023.

Pantoja, who only lost the belt in an injury-induced defeat last December, recalled after his title win: “Money was short, and my wife started to clean houses, and I started to drive Uber Eats.

“But I’d do this again if I needed, you know? It’s not just about myself, this is about my family and my kids – my two boys.”

title: Ray tracing massive amounts of animated geometry using tetrahedral cages
description: Animate compact tetrahedral cages and reuse static mini-BLASes to ray-trace hundreds of millions of triangles in real time, dramatically cutting per-frame update and memory costs for dense foliage, grass, and crowds.
fullText:
Ray tracing has become a practical part of real-time graphics, but animated geometry still presents a difficult scaling problem. The paper Ray Tracing Massive Amounts of Animated Geometry by Gruen et al., winner of the third-place Wolfgang Straßer Award (Best Paper) at High-Performance Graphics 2026, tackles a common production case: scenes with many complex objects that all deform differently. In a conventional ray-tracing pipeline, every uniquely animated mesh may require both its vertices and its acceleration structure to be updated each frame. That computational cost grows with triangle density, so geometrically dense foliage, grass, crowds, or creatures can quickly consume the frame budget. As each unique animated object may need to store its own set of bounding volume hierarchy, memory consumption may become prohibitively high.

Figure 1. Animated scene with roughly 585 million animated triangles @ 60 frames per second (AMD Radeon™ RX 9070 XT GPU at 1080p)

The central idea of the paper is to decouple animation cost from triangle count. Instead of applying animation directly to every triangle, the method builds a low-resolution tetrahedral cage around the original, non-animated mesh. During preprocessing, the mesh is split into small, disjoint pieces associated with the tetrahedra. Static mini-BLASes are then built for these pieces once and reused. At runtime, only the cage is animated. Rays that enter an animated tetrahedron are transformed into the corresponding rest-pose space, where they can intersect the static geometry. The resulting motion is an approximation: vertex movement is represented by a piecewise-linear deformation induced by the cage, rather than by a smooth global bend or by full per-vertex animation.

This makes the approach especially attractive for massive scenes where many copies of the same asset are animated in unique ways. Each unique deformation needs its own animated cage, but it can share the same rest-pose mini-meshes and mini-BLASes. The paper demonstrates this with trees, grass patches, and frogs, and shows a combined scene of roughly 585 million animated triangles rendered at 60 frames per second on an AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT graphics card at 1080p. The key message is not that tetrahedral cages replace every animation technique, but that they provide an additional new low-cost option when acceleration-structure updates, animation time, or memory footprint become the bottleneck. The paper also sits alongside similar independent work by Luton and Tricard (2026), which explores tetrahedral indirection but uses multiple hardware rays and no clipping step. Please note that tetrahedron-cage driven animations can be combined with the Microsoft DirectX® Raytracing (DXR) Functional Spec, Part 2 – e.g. partitioned top-level acceleration structures – and can also coexist with cluster level acceleration structures.

The trade-off is control over quality versus performance. A coarser cage is cheaper and smaller, while a finer cage can follow the original animation more closely. The method is best suited to connectivity-preserving animation, such as swaying vegetation, grass, crowds, distant characters, or animation level of detail. It is less appropriate for cases where topology changes, very small-scale motion, or sharp character deformations are the visual focus. Please note though that tetrahedron-cage driven animations can also be used in the context of partitioned top-level structures and can also coexist with cluster level acceleration structures.

Overall, the paper presents tetrahedral cages as a practical bridge between rich animated content and hardware-accelerated ray tracing. By animating a compact proxy and reusing static rest-pose geometry, it opens the door to scenes that would otherwise be too expensive to update every frame. Future work will focus on improving cage generation and animation quality, as well as integration with modern ray-tracing pipelines. Please note that we are hard at work to create DXR samples and a header-only C++ library that allows building high-quality tetrahedral cages for skinned objects, keyframe-animated objects, and static objects. If you’re working on animated geometry at scale, we encourage you to dive into the full paper below, and stay tuned to GPUOpen as this research continues to evolve.

Links to third party sites are provided for convenience and unless explicitly stated, AMD is not responsible for the contents of such linked sites and no endorsement is implied. GD-97.

DirectX is a trademark of the Microsoft group of companies.

title: Apple Maps to power navigation experience for Ford UEV Platform
description: Apple and Ford announced Apple Maps will be integrated directly into Ford’s UEV Platform through the MapKit for Automotive SDK.
fullText:
UPDATE

 July 23, 2026

 Apple Maps to power the navigation experience for the Ford Universal Electric Vehicle Platform

 Apple and Ford today announced that Apple Maps will be integrated directly into Ford’s upcoming Universal Electric Vehicle (UEV) Platform through Apple’s new MapKit for Automotive SDK. Coming to Ford’s UEV Platform in 2027, this integration will deliver a beautiful and easy-to-use navigation experience powered by Apple Maps directly to the vehicle’s displays. Road-level Maps information will also enable Ford’s Latitude AI team to build a seamless hands-free driving experience.

 Leveraging Apple’s new MapKit for Automotive SDK, Ford’s Universal Electric Vehicle Platform will offer drivers turn-by-turn directions using natural language, real-time traffic and incident information, intuitive search featuring detailed place cards, and routing options to help users easily find the best route to their destination. Additionally, Ford UEV drivers will benefit from an intelligent EV routing functionality, including battery preconditioning. Ford will also use road-level information from Apple Maps as part of the development of its next-generation BlueCruise hands-free highway driving capability to deliver a more seamless on-ramp-to-off-ramp experience.

 “Apple Maps delivers the best map experience in the world, and we’re excited to bring the power of Maps’ navigation technology to Ford’s innovative Universal Electric Vehicle Platform,” said Eddy Cue, Apple’s senior vice president of Services and Health. “With our new MapKit for Automotive SDK, we’re bringing Maps further into drivers’ daily lives, giving them an incredibly accurate and easy-to-use navigation system that is seamlessly integrated into Ford vehicles.”

 “Our new midsize electric vehicle will be priced around $30,000 and redefines what advanced technology can be — simple, useful, and truly attainable for more customers,” said Jim Farley, Ford Motor Company’s CEO. “We’re proud to embed Apple Maps’ navigation and mapping technology directly into our Universal Electric Vehicle Platform, giving customers the ultimate navigation experience alongside our Ford app, a full suite of software, and next-generation BlueCruise, all enabled by a new zonal architecture. Apple Maps has delivered a world-class product, and we’re honored to be among the first to embed it directly into a vehicle, helping define intuitive, capable driving.”

 This integration is made possible by Apple’s new MapKit for Automotive SDK, which allows automakers to embed and customize Apple Maps. The SDK also enables automakers to deliver a cohesive navigation experience for drivers that is consistent with the look and feel of their vehicles, and provides road-level information to help automakers build hands-free driving experiences. The SDK follows the same privacy principles that the Apple Maps app provides today. Apple does not collect users’ location details and activity on the vehicle’s map in a way that can be tied back to the individual user.

 In addition to the integration of Apple Maps into Ford’s UEV Platform, CarPlay will continue to be available on Ford vehicles, providing drivers with a safe and smart way to use their iPhone in the car.
